Introduction
Document scope
1.1 Document scope
The corrective maintenance manual (CMM) helps service engineers troubleshoot, repair and
maintain the systems, subsystems and components of the linear accelerator. The target audience
is qualified service engineers. These may be engineers employed directly by Elekta, qualified
representatives of Elekta or qualified representatives of Elekta's customers.
The manual provides:
• A description of the system or sub-system in question.
• A troubleshooting section to identify the problem areas.
• A set of detailed procedures used to replace FRUs (field replaceable units).
• A section on how to return the device to work.
If you find any mistakes in this document, or identify areas for improvement, please raise a CLM
(customer lifecycle management) case and assign it to the relevant queue.
1.2 Intended users
Table 1.1 Definition of persons who can use or work on the equipment
Term Definition
Authorized A person that is permitted by the user to use or do work on the equipment by
person the User of the equipment.
Note: The authorized person must have the correct level of knowledge and
training before using or doing work on the equipment.
A person that is approved by Elekta to have the necessary knowledge and
Elekta approved training to do specified tasks on Elekta equipment.
person Note: The authorized person must have the correct level of knowledge and
training before using or doing work on the equipment.
Operator A person who operates the equipment with or without help from an assistant,
and who controls some or all the functions of the equipment.
Note: The operator must have the correct level of knowledge and training
before using or doing work on the equipment.
Clinical User A person who operates the equipment for the treatment of patients.
Note: A Clinical User must be a qualified person who has the necessary
knowledge and training in the safe, clinical operation of the equipment. Such
treatment must be therapeutic only.
Clinical User – The role of Radiation Therapists, also referred to as Radiotherapists, Radiation
Radiation Treatment Technicians or Therapeutic/Therapy radiographers, may be
Therapist different based on their geographical region and experience/qualifications.
However, their primary roles and obligations are expected to be similar among
different regions and sites.
Note: Radiation Therapists are trained on all aspects of system operation, but
not trained on installation, calibration or service maintenance.
Note: Radiation Therapists who work at the site when the system is installed
will be trained by an Elekta trainer. After the system is introduced, Elekta
authorized trainers, or experienced Radiation Therapists who are already
trained on the equipment, can train Radiation Therapists who are new to the
equipment.

Introduction
Intended users
Term Definition
Clinical User – Dosimetrists design, generate, and measure radiation dose distributions and
Medical dose calculations in collaboration with the Medical Physics Expert and
Dosimetrist Radiation Oncologist.
Note: Dosimetrists are trained on the general principles of system operation,
but not trained on installation, calibration, or service maintenance.
Medical Physics A person that is recognized by a competent authority to have the necessary
Expert (MPE) knowledge and training in radiation physics, or radiation treatment, to give
Radiation information on patient dosimetry, use of radiation procedures and equipment,
Protection on optimization, quality assurance, do the applicable tests and calculations for
Advisor (RPA) radiation levels and dose assessments, and other items related to medical
radiation exposure and safety.
Note: Medical Physicists are trained on performing system QA procedures and
calibration or service maintenance tasks.
Radiation A physician whose trained medical specialty is the use of radiation therapy as
Oncologist a treatment for cancer.
Radiotherapy A Nurse with specific focus on the Radiation Oncology clinic patient support.
Nurse Certain countries utilize specially trained nurses to run the Linear Accelerator.
Qualified person A person that is recognized by a competent authority to have the necessary
knowledge and training to do specified tasks.
Note: The qualified person must have the correct level of knowledge and
training before using or doing work on the equipment.
Qualified Expert A person that is recognized by a competent authority to do the applicable
(QE) tests and calculations for radiation levels and dose assessments. The person
also provides information on the necessary correct radiation protection and
procedures.
Note: The qualified expert must have the correct level of knowledge and
training before using or doing work on the equipment.
Service User - A Service User operates the equipment to do maintenance tasks. A Service
Service engineer User is a qualified person who has the necessary knowledge and training to
set different software configurations, do the necessary tests, adjustments,
optimization, and calibration procedures on the equipment. Such operation
must not be therapeutic.
Note: The Service User must have the correct level of knowledge and training
before using or doing work on the equipment.
User The organization or person responsible for the operation and maintenance of
the equipment.
Note: The user must have the correct level of knowledge and training before
using or doing work on the equipment.
For more information on persons that can work on the equipment, and on training, contact your
local Elekta representative.

1.4 Glossary of terms
Table 1.3 Glossary of terms
Term Definition
Autotrack function The autotrack function automatically moves the diaphragms or
leaves to their prescribed position.
Cal block The Cal block contains the item and part values that, when
loaded, configure the linear accelerator for a given energy.
Clinical Mode In Clinical Mode, Clinical Users use the linear accelerator and its
accessories for the radiation therapy treatment that a licensed
medical practitioner prescribes.
Gating Gating is the procedure by which an external system or manual
task pauses and starts the radiation delivery as a result of
information received relating to the treatment position of the
patient.
Interdigitation Interdigitation is an overlap of the opposite adjacent leaves of a
multileaf collimator.
Service life Service life is the length of time that the regular servicing and
repair procedures can keep the machine in operation.
Service Mode In Service Mode, Service Users do the necessary maintenance
tasks and calibration of the equipment.

General safety information
Important safety instructions
2.1 Important safety instructions
It is important to install, operate, maintain, and dispose of the equipment according to the
information in the product documentation, and in compliance with local regulations. For the
safety of the users and the patients, before a user operates or works on the equipment, they
must read, understand, and obey all:
• Warnings, cautions, instructions, and notes in this document and the related
documents.
• Safety instructions, emergency procedures, and information in this document and the
related documents.
• Warnings, cautions, and safety markings on the equipment and the accessories of the
equipment.
If you have local work instructions for the equipment, then Elekta recommends that you include
the warnings, cautions, and notes from this document in your local work instructions.
2.1.1 Conventions for warnings, cautions, and notes
The conventions give information about the class and the level of the risk in the warnings and
cautions, and the content of notes.
WARNING 2.1
A warning is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of fatal or serious
injury, injury, or clinical mistreatment, if you do not obey the instructions.
CAUTION 2.1
A caution is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of damage to the
equipment, data, or the environment, if you do not obey the instructions.
Note: A note gives more information about a related text.
2.1.2 Expected service life of the equipment
The expected service life is the length of time that the regular service and repair procedures can
keep the equipment safe to operate.
WARNING 2.2
Do not operate the equipment unless you correctly complete the safety checks, planned
maintenance schedules, and extended life checks. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
The expected service life of this equipment is 15 years of normal use.
The Elekta definition for 'expected service life' is the length of time that the regular service and
repair procedures can keep the equipment safe to operate.
The Elekta definition of 'normal use' is the treatment of 50 patients each day for five days each
week of the year. If the equipment is used more frequently than this, the frequency of the
planned maintenance must increase accordingly and the extended life checks must also be
brought forward accordingly.

General safety information
Important safety instructions
The level of operation of the equipment can be different to ‘normal use’. This has an effect on the
length of the service life. Because of wear of parts, the risk of a malfunction can increase after 10
years operation. Elekta recommends that before you continue operation after 10 years, you
complete the regular extended life checks.
Elekta can help the User with the correct safety checks, planned maintenance schedules, and the
regular extended life checks for continued safe operation of the equipment.
The system must be maintained throughout its expected service life in compliance with the EMC
information detailed in the applicable Instructions for Use.
For information on the above checks and schedules, contact your local Elekta representative.

2.2 Safety precautions
2.2.1 Safety precautions for the compatibility of the equipment
WARNING 2.3
Do not use parts, accessories, or other equipment, without approval by Elekta of their
compatibility. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury and damage to the
equipment.
Only operate the equipment with Elekta supplied or approved, compatible equipment or parts.
Accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by or do not have the approval of
Elekta can cause an incorrect treatment delivery. They can also have an effect on the performance
of the electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), which can increase the emissions or decrease the
immunity of the equipment.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of other equipment or parts.
2.2.2 Safety precautions for the electromagnetic compatibility
The design and manufacture of Elekta products is in compliance with applicable EMC standards.
The intended result of the standards is to minimize the risk of electromagnetic interference by RF
devices. The type of device and the distance to the equipment has an effect on the intensity of
the electromagnetic interference. The equipment includes the linear accelerator and accessories,
the control cabinets, the computers in the control room, and equipment from other
manufacturer.

Page 18 © Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved.

General safety information
Safety precautions
It is necessary to have special precautions for EMC emissions. Elekta recommends that you obey
these precautions when you use RF devices near the equipment.
• Do not use RF devices unless they are in compliance with international standards for
electromagnetic compatibility, for example, the CE or FCC mark.
• Do not use a two-way emergency radio in the treatment room or the control room.
Only use an emergency radio if there is an emergency.
• Do not use cellular telephones, or other devices with cellular modems near the
equipment. The Instructions for Use contains information on the minimum safe
distance of these devices from the equipment.
• When you use a device that is enabled for wireless operation, it must be a minimum of
10 cm from the equipment.
• Radio-Frequency Identification (RFID) readers, when activated to do a scan of a RFID
tag, must be a minimum of one meter from the equipment.
Note: The RFID systems that Elekta supply use lower power levels. You can safely use these RFID systems
when you obey the procedures in the Instructions for Use for the product.
The system must be installed, put into service, and maintained in compliance with the EMC
information in the Instructions for Use.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of parts and equipment.
WARNING 2.4
Do not put portable or mobile radio frequency (RF) communication devices near to the
equipment. The emissions from such RF devices can be more than the EMC standards, and can
have unwanted effects on the equipment operation. If you ignore this warning, the RF
emissions can cause the equipment to have a malfunction. A malfunction can cause fatal injury,
clinical mistreatment, or damage to the equipment.
WARNING 2.5
Do not use accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by Elekta. They can have
an effect on the EMC performance, which can increase EMC emissions or decrease the
immunity to RF interference of the equipment. If you ignore this warning, incorrect radiation
delivery and other malfunctions can occur, which can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
2.2.3 Safety precaution for Service Tools and lifting equipment
WARNING 2.6
Do not use defective or damaged lifting equipment. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 2.7
Do not use incorrectly calibrated or defective Service Tools. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause injury and damage to the equipment.
Before you use the Service Tool or lifting equipment, make sure that:
• There are no defective or damaged parts
• The calibration of the Service Tool is correct

General safety information
Safety precautions
• The applicable calibration certificate is valid
• The lifting equipment gives the maximum Safe Working Load (SWL).
For information on the maintenance and calibration schedules of the Service Tool, see the
Instructions for Use and Calibration Certificate for the tool.
2.2.4 Safety precaution for work with electrical and mechanical parts
WARNING 2.8
Do not connect any electrical power supply before the ground earth connections are secure.
There is a risk of high leakage current. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 2.9
Do not remove the cables or the covers, or operate the equipment without the covers. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury
The covers on the equipment prevent access to high voltage parts and parts that move. If you
remove the covers, there is a risk of high voltage shock or injury from parts that move.
Only remove the covers if you are an Elekta approved person with the correct authority and you
obey the applicable safety procedures.
2.2.5 Safety precaution for work with safety devices during service
operation
WARNING 2.10
Do not remove, change, or override the safety switches, interlocks, and other safety devices. If
you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
The procedures in this document can tell you to remove, change, or override the safety switches,
interlocks, or other safety devices during repair or maintenance work. When the work is
completed, make all the safety devices active again, and do a test of them.
2.2.6 Safety precaution for work on BLDs with UV lighting
WARNING 2.11
Do not look into the ultraviolet lighting of the BLD without protective glasses. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause serious injury.
The covers and screens of the BLD prevent an injury to your eyes from the ultraviolet lighting.
Before you remove the covers or screens of the BLD, switch off the linear accelerator, or use
protective glasses. Protective glasses must have the UV-A specification OD 6+ @ 190 - 532 Nm.
If there is damage to the covers or screen of the BLD, switch off the linear accelerator and contact
the Elekta representative.

General safety information
Safety precautions
2.2.7 Safety precaution for work with lasers
WARNING 2.12
Do not look into the laser beams because they can cause damage to your eyes. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause serious injury.
Elekta systems use the laser beams to help set up patients in the correct position for treatment.
During your work in the treatment room, make sure that you do not look into the laser beams.
2.2.8 Safety precautions for work with display screen equipment
WARNING 2.13
Do not use the display screen equipment for long periods of time. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause injury.
When you use the display screen equipment (DSE), obey the applicable Health and Safety
regulations and procedures in the jurisdiction where you do the work.
2.2.9 Safety precautions for cleaning and disinfection
WARNING 2.14
Do not clean the equipment, or use a disinfectant spray when the equipment is connected to
the electrical supply. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 2.15
Do not use a spray that is explosive or flammable. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 2.2
Do not let water and other liquids go into the equipment. If you ignore this caution, you can
cause damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 2.3
Do not use a spray to clean the medical equipment room. If you ignore this caution, you can
cause damage to the equipment.
At regular intervals, it is necessary to clean the equipment and equipment room with
disinfectants. Elekta recommends that the procedures to clean the equipment and the equipment
room obey the applicable laws and regulations of the jurisdiction where the equipment is
installed.

General safety information
Safety precautions
2.2.10 Safety precautions for work with high temperatures on the BLD
covers
During normal operation, the maximum temperatures of the accessory ring and BLD outer covers
can increase to 45 °C. Make sure that you do not touch the outer covers or the accessory ring for
more than 1 minute.
2.2.11 Safety precaution for the installation of accessories on the BLD
WARNING 2.16
Do not touch the connector found in the accessory ring on the BLD. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause injury.
When you install an accessory, for example, a shadow tray, electron applicator, or front pointer,
do not touch the pins in the connector. The pins have a low electrical current, which can give an
electric shock when you touch them.
2.2.12 Safety precautions for work with heavy parts
The safety precautions for work with heavy parts are the necessary safety precautions to know to
prevent injury.
WARNING 2.17
Do not lift or move heavy parts unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety regulations
and procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
2.2.13 Safety precautions during work on the BLD
When you do work on the BLD, make sure that you obey the following safety precautions:
WARNING 2.18
Do not do work on the inner parts of the beam limiting device while the electrical supply is
connected. There is a risk of high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 2.19
Do not do work on the beam limiting device unless the gantry is at 0° or 180°. Gravity can cause
the leaves, diaphragms, and dynamic leaf guides to move. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 2.20
Do not touch the hot LED field light projector, or the heat sink, immediately after operation. If
you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.

General safety information
Safety precautions
2.2.14 Safety precaution for the removal of parts from the radiation head
WARNING 2.21
Do not touch the flight tube, bending magnet, or other parts near to them until a check for
induced radioactivity is completed. Excessive exposure to radioactivity is dangerous. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
After the operation of the linear accelerator at high energy, the parts in the radiation head can
become radioactive and a radiation hazard. Before you do work on, or remove parts from the
radiation head, use a calibrated radiation detector to measure the level of induced radioactivity.
Use the applicable safety procedures when you remove radioactive parts and material. The
maximum dose rate for the safe removal of radioactive parts and material is 50 µSv/hr
(microsievert per hour). Do not remove the radioactive parts and material from the treatment
room before they are correctly prepared for safe removal.
2.2.15 Safety precautions for maintenance, modification, and
decommissioning
WARNING 2.22
Do not do work, or make modifications to the equipment without the correct training and
authority. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and
damage to the equipment.
Only do work on, or make modifications to, the equipment if you are an Elekta approved person
with the applicable authority. Such work must use best practice and obey the laws and
regulations in the jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed.
Modifications or work that does not have approval by Elekta, and is not done by an Elekta
approved person with the correct authority, can cause:
• Fatal injury
• Incorrect radiation delivery
• Malfunction of the equipment
• Damage to the equipment
• The warranty to be canceled.
2.2.16 Safety precautions for work in areas of induced radioactivity
The safety precautions for work in areas of induced radioactivity are the precautions to prevent
an excessive exposure to, and serious injury from, induced radioactivity.
When you operate or do work on the equipment, Elekta recommends that you use the applicable
radiation protection functions, devices, systems, procedures, and accessories. Always obey the
applicable laws and regulations for induced radioactivity for the jurisdiction in which the
equipment is installed.
The safety precautions are applicable to:
• Maintenance, modifications, and decommissioning
• Measurement of the induced radioactivity
• Removal and movement of parts with induced radioactivity.

General safety information
Safety precautions
2.2.16.1 Safety precautions for the measurement of induced radioactivity
The safety precautions for the measurement of induced radioactivity are the necessary safety
precautions to know to prevent excessive exposure to radioactivity, which can cause serious
injury or damage to the environment.
Always use two radiation detectors to measure the level of induced radioactivity. Only use
radiation detectors that have an applicable calibration certificate. The period of time since the
last calibration date must be less than 12 months. Do not use the radiation detectors if the period
of time since the last calibration date is more than 12 months.
WARNING 2.23
Do not move radioactive material (by air, sea, or land) unless you obey the applicable laws and
regulations for induced radioactivity and disposal of radioactive material of the jurisdictions
through which the movement occurs. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury
and damage to the environment.
2.2.16.2 Safety precautions for handling parts with induced radioactivity
The handling of parts with induced radioactivity must comply with the laws and regulations of the
jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed. The safe levels of radiation applicable in the UK
do not apply in all jurisdictions. The permitted level of radioactivity must always be as low as
reasonably achievable (ALARA). This level may vary depending on local rules, therefore the safe
working levels from the applicable local rules must be considered as the acceptable working limit.
If there are no safe levels available for a jurisdiction, use the UK value as a guideline, which is a
maximum of 150 cps at the surface for a maximum duration of 72 minutes. The following table
shows the conversion from this value to other units.

Do not use the equipment in rooms that contain explosive or flammable gases. If you ignore

High levels of oxygen, and some anesthetic gases and disinfectant sprays, can make an explosive

The regulations for training give a description of the necessary training, regulations, and

Different countries have different regulations for training. Make sure that you have the necessary

3.1.3 Disposal of the equipment
End of life (EOL) disposal is the procedure to disassemble, remove, and discard the equipment, in
full or in parts, to a condition where it is not possible to operate the equipment for its intended
use.
Only a Qualified Expert (QE) and an approved facility with an applicable license, may remove and
discard the equipment and recycle the material.
A full description of disposal requirements is detailed in a separate document Product Disposal
Information. This document is available on request from Elekta.
The customer owns the data on the storage media. If there are any data storage media such as
HDDs, remove them from the device and hand them to the customer for disposal. If the customer
entrusts the disposal team to dispose of the data, the data must be destroyed in accordance with
relevant cybersecurity regulations.

Product description
Cooling water, gas and vacuum systems on the linear accelerator
4.1 Cooling water, gas and vacuum systems on the linear
accelerator
The linear accelerator has internal and external cooling water systems to keep high temperature
parts of the linear accelerator at a specified temperature.
The internal cooling water system has a water pump that moves distilled water around the system
to remove the heat from the high temperature parts. The warm distilled water flows through the
heat exchanger on the cooling water unit, where the temperature decreases before it moves back
around the system. The heat exchanger is also connected to the external water system, where
cold water from the hospital water supply or a water chiller moves through the heat exchanger to
cool the water in the internal water system. The warm water in the external water system flows
from the heat exchanger to the hospital drain or to the input of the water chiller.
The capacity of the internal cooling water system is 18 L (4 UK gallons, 4.8 US gallons), but
approximately 25 L (5.5 UK gallons, 6.6 US gallons) are necessary to fill and bleed the system.
The rectangular waveguide on the linear accelerator contains the dielectric gas. The dielectric gas
keeps the waveguide cool and prevents high voltage discharge. An external gas cylinder with a gas
regulator supplies the dielectric gas to a valve in AREA 60 or on the RIC. The TCS can open the
valve when the linear accelerator starts and automatically fill the waveguide with the dielectric
gas. If the gas pressure is too high, the pressure release valve that is set at 1.5 bar prevents
damage to the waveguide. The valve is set at the factory or when the linear accelerator is
installed.
The linear accelerator waveguide is evacuated to a specified vacuum. This prevents collisions
between electrons and gas molecules that can cause the electrons to change direction or stop. To
evacuate to the specified vacuum, the waveguide is first evacuated with the use of a rotary vane
pump or a turbomolecular pump. For the linear accelerators with the Varian ion pumps, Elekta
recommends that you use the turbomolecular pump to evacuate the waveguide, because this
pump makes a better vacuum. When the waveguide is at the correct vacuum, two ion pumps
keep the vacuum at the specified level for the correct operation of the linear accelerator. One of
the ion pumps is at the target end of the waveguide and the second ion pump is at the gun end.
The vacuum pressure at the gun end and target end of the waveguide is displayed on the screen
displays in the control room and the treatment room.
The TCS uses the software items to monitor and control the cooling water, gas and vacuum
systems.
4.2 Cooling water system
4.2.1 Description of the external cooling water system
The function of the external cooling water system is to:
• Supply the cold water to the heat exchanger on the cooling water unit from the
primary water supply of the hospital or the water chiller.
• Send the warm water from the heat exchanger to the drain system of the hospital or
the inlet of the water chiller.
Most of the parts of the external cooling water circuit are in the cable reeling post (AREA 60) or
the RIC in the equipment room.
Not controlled when downloaded or printed
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 45133702093_11
Corrective Maintenance Manual - Cooling, Gas, and Vacuum Systems
© Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved. Page 37

Product description
Cooling water system
4.2.1.1 Description of the external cooling water inlet
The inlet of the external cooling water supply is connected to the external cooling water system at
the cable reeling post or the RIC.
The inlet side of the external cooling water system has:
• A manual stop valve to isolate the external cooling water supply during maintenance.
• A coarse strainer to keep out unwanted material.
• A pressure gauge to show the pressure of the external cooling water supply.
• A connection to the external inlet side of the heat exchanger on the cooling water unit.
4.2.1.2 Description of the external cooling water outlet
The outlet side of the external cooling water system has:
• A connection from the external outlet side of the heat exchanger to a three-way valve
with a motor or actuator control.
Note: The three-way valve increases or decreases the cold water flow through the heat exchanger to
control the water temperature of the internal cooling water system.
• A pressure gauge that shows the outlet pressure of the water from external cooling
water system.
• A 1 bar (15 psi) pressure release valve between the inlet and outlet pipes of the
external cooling water supply.
Note: The pressure release valve changes the direction of the flow of the external water supply when the
three-way valve is closed.
• A manual stop valve to isolate the external cooling water system during maintenance.
• A connection from the manual stop valve to the external cooling supply.
If the external cooling water supply is from the hospital primary water supply, the outlet connects
to the hospital drain system. If the external cooling water supply is from a water chiller, the outlet
connects to the inlet of the water chiller.
4.2.2 Internal cooling water system
The internal cooling water system is a closed loop, pressurized system that contains distilled
water. It has a primary supply circuit and a number of smaller circuits that go to different parts of
the linear accelerator. The distilled water removes heat from the high temperature parts of the
linear accelerator and then flows through the heat exchanger on the cooling water unit. In the
heat exchanger, heat transfers from the distilled water to the water in the external water system.
The pipes of the internal water system have a layer of the chemical benzotriazole on the inner
part of the pipes. This prevents the growth of microorganisms. The benzotriazole is installed at
the factory and stays active for the life of the linear accelerator.
An electric pump in AREA 60 or on the RIC fills the internal cooling water system with distilled
water from a container. As the internal cooling water system fills with water, it is necessary to
bleed the air out of the system. The bleed valves are on the cooling water unit and the digital
accelerator. It is important to remove as much air as possible for correct operation of the cooling
water system.

water through the system.

Product description
Cooling water system
From the supply manifold, the cooling water flows to these parts in the gantry arm:
• The waveguide injector and the magnetron
• The bending magnet assembly
• The waveguide relativistic section.
A thermal switch and a temperature sensor on the supply manifold monitor the temperature of
the water in the internal cooling water system. The TCS displays this temperature as software

4.2.2.6 Supply manifold on the cooling water unit on the linear accelerator from serial
number 152178 to 152971
The outlet of the secondary side (internal water system) of the heat exchanger connects to the
input of the supply manifold.
From the supply manifold, the cooling water flows to these parts in the gantry arm:
• The waveguide injector and the magnetron
• The bending magnet assembly
• The waveguide relativistic section.
A thermal switch and a temperature sensor on the supply manifold monitor the temperature of
the water in the internal cooling water system. If the water temperature increases above a
specified level, the thermal switch sends an inhibit signal to the TCS. The temperature controller
on the cooling water unit uses the sensor to control the temperature of the water.
There is a valve on the supply manifold to help bleed air from the internal cooling water system.
4.2.2.7 Supply manifold on the cooling water unit for the linear accelerator from serial
number 152972
The outlet of the secondary side (internal water system) of the heat exchanger connects to the
input of the supply manifold.
From the supply manifold, the cooling water flows to these parts in the gantry arm:
• The waveguide injector and the magnetron
• The bending magnet assembly
• The waveguide relativistic section.
A thermal switch on the supply manifold monitors the temperature of the water in the internal
water system. If the water temperature goes above a specified level, the thermal switch sends an
inhibit signal to the TCS.
There is a valve on the supply manifold to help bleed air from the internal cooling water system.
4.2.2.8 Description of the water pump on the cooling water unit
The water pump on the cooling water unit moves distilled water through the internal water
system at a flow of 67 L/min (14.74 gals/min) and a pressure of 0.9 bar (13 psi). There are low
pressure and high pressure switches in the pump inlet line to keep the flow and pressure in
specified limits. An inhibit occurs if the flow or the pressure is not in these limits.
The outlet of the water pump is connected to the secondary side (internal water system) of the
heat exchanger. The outlet includes a drain valve to help remove water from the internal cooling
water system for maintenance.
The contactors and circuit breakers in the control circuit are in the interface cabinet (AREA 70) or
in the RIC. The contacts of low pressure switch SW41-1 are connected in parallel with contacts

Product description
Cooling water system
21/22 of circuit breaker CB11 (water pump). These contacts are the 24 V AC supply to the primary
system contactor CON-A.
If the circuit breaker CB11 (water pump) is off:
• The contacts 21/22 are closed.
• The water pump is isolated from its three-phase supply, but contacts 21/22 of CB11
bypass the pressure switch and it is possible to energize CON-A.
If the circuit breaker CB11 (water pump) is on:
• The contacts 21/22 are open.
• The contacts TS13A 1, 2 and 3 apply the three-phase supply to the pump (if CON-A is
on).
• The low pressure switch is in series with the coil of CON-A.
The shaft seal of the water pump is damaged immediately if the pump runs dry. To help prevent
this, the pump has a protective secondary low pressure switch in the interlock chain to the
primary system contactor CON-A. The secondary low pressure switch operates when the water
pressure is less than 0.2 bar (2.9 psi).
There is a thermal switch, attached to the outer body of the water pump, which gives protection
against high temperatures. The thermal switch is in the interlock chain to the primary system
contactor CON-A. If the temperature of the water pump is more than 90 °C, the thermal switch
operates and opens CON-A.
Note: There is no thermal switch in the cooling unit water pump installed on linear accelerators after
serial number 154222 and on machines which have the Cooling unit water pump upgrade kit FRU
applied.
The water pump can operate with a 50 Hz or 60 Hz supply. With a 60 Hz supply, the pump
operates at a high speed, which causes the flow and pressure to increase in the internal water
system. To keep the flow and pressure in specified limits, a flow restrictor is installed in the outlet
of the water pump. For a 50 Hz supply, it is not necessary to install the flow restrictor.
4.2.2.9 Description of the expansion vessel on the cooling water unit
The expansion vessel lets the volume of the cooling water increase and decrease when the water
pressure and water temperature change. The internal air pressure of the expansion vessel must
be between 0.5 ± 0.1 bar (7.25 ± 1.45 psi).
4.2.2.10 Description of the push-fit water connections
Push-fit connections replace the compression connections on the pipes that connect to the
cooling water unit. This helps to decrease the installation time. The machine side connections
continue to be compression connections.
4.2.2.11 Description of the return manifold on the cooling water unit
The water pipes from each part of the internal water system connect to the return manifold. The
outlet of the return manifold goes to the water pump.
The distilled water that flows to the return manifold comes from the:
• Magnetron
• Mode transformer
• Focus coils

4.2.2.12 Description of the soft start module
A soft start module is fitted which increases the pump speed slowly to avoid a pressure drop that
might cause contactor A to de-energise.
4.2.2.13 Description of the waveguide injector and the magnetron water system
The distilled water from the supply manifold flows through the waveguide injector (AREA 20) and
magnetron (AREA 30) water circuit to the return manifold. This water circuit helps keep cool the
waveguide injector and the magnetron during usual operation.
To isolate the magnetron from the water circuit for maintenance, there is a manual stop valve
between the waveguide injector and the magnetron. There is also a manual stop valve between
the magnetron and return manifold. These two valves are the same type of valve. The valve on
the input to the magnetron has a screw to bleed air from the water system.
A flow switch between the magnetron and the return manifold monitors the flow of distilled
water through the water system.
4.2.2.14 Description of the cooling water system for the bending magnet
The cooling water flows from the second outlet on the water supply manifold through the
bending magnet assembly (AREA 23). From the bending magnet assembly, the cooling water
flows to an inlet on the water return manifold.
The cooling water system for the bending magnet assembly has two filters that remove unwanted
material from the water:
• A 5 micron filter (F1) in the inlet pipe
• A 5 micron filter (F2) in the outlet pipe.
4.2.2.15 Description of the waveguide relativistic water system
The cooling water from the supply manifold flows through the waveguide relativistic section in to
four water systems. The four water systems cool eight different parts of the linear accelerator.
The cooling water flows to the return manifold from the:

4.2.2.16 Description of the equipment to fill the internal cooling water system for linear
accelerators to 152783
The equipment to fill the internal cooling water system are supplied with the linear accelerator on
the cable reeling post (AREA 60). The equipment is:
• An electric pump with a pressure switch and a one-way valve on the outlet
• A pressure gauge
• Two manual stop valves.
The electric pump fills the internal cooling water system through the one-way valve from a
distilled water container. The pressure switch stops the electric pump when the internal cooling
water system is full. The electric pump power switch is on the junction box at the top of the cable
reeling post.
After the one-way valve, the flow of the distilled water can move in two directions. Each direction
has a manual stop valve.
• If the fill valve is open, distilled water flows to the return manifold on the cooling water
unit to fill or add the water to the internal cooling water system.
• If the drain valve is open, distilled water flows to the distilled water container. This
valve is closed during usual operation.
4.2.2.17 Description of the equipment to fill the internal cooling water system for linear
accelerators from 152784
The equipment to fill the internal cooling water system is supplied with the digital accelerator on
the RIC. The equipment is:
• An electric pump with a pressure switch on the outlet
• Two manual stop valves.
The electric pump fills the internal cooling water system from a distilled water container. The
pressure switch stops the electric pump when the internal cooling water system is full. The
electric pump power switch (SW2-WP) is on the left side panel of the RIC.

Cooling water system
The outlet of the electric pump connects to the valve assembly. From here the distilled water can

• If the fill valve is open, distilled water flows to the return manifold on the cooling water

4.2.3 Control of the cooling water system
4.2.3.1 Description and operation of the control circuit of the internal cooling water
system
The water pressure in the internal cooling water system can change during the operation of the
digital accelerator. The expansion vessel absorbs small changes, which do not stop the operation
of the cooling water system or the digital accelerator.
If a fault occurs or air gets in the system, there can be a large change in the pressure. The control
circuit operates two low pressure switches and one high pressure switch. The switches cause an
inhibit when a fault occurs which stops the operation of the water pump and the digital
accelerator.
The high pressure switch (SW41-2) opens when the pressure increases to more than 1 bar
(14.5 psi). Software item i197 of the TCS senses this and stops the HT. This inhibit appears in the
inhibit list of the TCS.
The first low pressure switch (SW41-3) opens when the water pressure decreases to less than
0.5 bar (7.25 psi). Software item i196 of the TCS senses this and stops the HT. This inhibit appears
in the inhibit list of the TCS.
If the water pressure decreases to less than 0.2 bar (2.9 psi), the second low pressure switch
(SW41-1) opens and de-energizes the primary system contactor CON-A.
4.2.3.2 Description of the flow switches in the cooling water system of linear
accelerators to 152177
The cooling water system has four flow switches that monitor the flow of the cooling water
through the:
• Waveguide injector to the magnetron.
• Waveguide relativistic section to the:
– Focus coils
– RF load, charge transformer, pulse transformer, magnetron magnet, and RF
isolator
– Target flight tube.
The flow switches measure the difference in pressure as the water flows through a calibrated hole
in the flow switch. The difference in pressure operates a flexible diaphragm that controls a
microswitch. When the water flow rate decreases, the pressure in the flow switch also decreases.
If the pressure in the flow switch goes below the calibrated setting, the microswitch gives a
warning of low water flow rate.
The flow switches operate the LT interlock circuit. When the flow rate is below the calibrated
setting, the flow switch operates and switches off the LT supply. An inhibit is displayed on the TCS.
For example, the calibration of the flow switch in the waveguide injector and magnetron water
system is set to measure a pressure decrease of 0.14 bar (2 psi) at a flow rate of 18 L/min (3.96
UK gallons/min, 4.76 US gallons/min).

The safe condition is a water flow rate that is approximately 25% above the flow rate at which the
switch is set to operate, for example at 22.5 L/min (4.95 UK gallons/min, 5.94 US gallons/min).
The safe water flow rate is 27 L/min (5.94 UK gallons/min, 7.13 US gallons/min).
Do not adjust the settings of the flow switches. The parameters of operation for the TRUFLO
switches are set by the manufacturer. If you change the settings of the flow switches, an incorrect
indication of the safe flow rate of the cooling water can occur.
4.2.3.3 Description of the flow switches in the cooling water system of linear
accelerators from 152178
The cooling water system has two flow switches that monitor the flow of the cooling water
through the:
• Waveguide injector to the magnetron.
• Waveguide relativistic section to the target flight tube.
The flow switches have a sensor that hangs in the water flow. When the water flow rate is above
the calibrated setting, the sensor moves and closes a magnetic switch. When the water flow rate
is below the calibrated setting, the sensor moves back to the start position and opens the switch.
The flow switches operate the LT interlock circuit. When the water flow rate is below the
calibrated setting, the switch opens. This switches off the LT supply and displays an inhibit on the
TCS. For example, the calibration of the flow switch in the waveguide injector and magnetron
water system is set to measure a pressure decrease of 0.14 bar (2 psi) at a flow rate of 18 L/min
(3.96 UK gallons/min, 4.76 US gallons/min).
The safe condition is a water flow rate that is approximately 25% above the water flow rate at
which the switch operates. For example, if the switch operates at 22.5 L/min (4.95 UK gallons/
min, 5.94 US gallon/min), the safe water flow rate is 27 L/min (5.94 UK gallons/min, 7.13 US
gallons/min).
Do not adjust the settings of the flow switches. The parameters of operation for the flow switches
are set by the manufacturer. If you change the settings of the flow switches, an incorrect
indication of the safe flow rate of the cooling water can occur.
4.2.3.4 Description of the three-way valve and actuator for linear accelerators from
serial number 152178
The three-way valve has two inputs A and B, and one output AB. Water in the external water
circuit flows directly to the A input of the valve. Water in the external water circuit that flows
through the heat exchanger goes to the B input of the valve. Water from the AB output flows to
the outlet of the external water circuit.
The temperature controller monitors the temperature of the internal water circuit with a K-type
thermocouple attached to the supply manifold. If the temperature increases, the controller
operates the actuator to open the B to AB path. This increases the flow of water through the heat
exchanger from the external water circuit. If the temperature decreases, the controller decreases
the flow of cool water through the B to AB path. If the temperature decreases sufficiently, the
controller fully closes the B to AB path. The cool water then flows through the A to AB path and
bypasses the heat exchanger.
4.2.3.5 Description of the temperature controller of linear accelerators from serial
number 152178 to 152971
The temperature controller is a rectangular device and is installed on the cooling water unit. Wire
connections for the power supply, thermocouple and actuator are made to terminals at the
bottom of the controller. There are two LCDs on the top that display actual temperature and set
temperature. The 24 VDC power supply to the controller comes from the UMD (AREA 11).

4.2.3.7 Description of the water temperature control for linear accelerators to serial
number 152177
When the linear accelerator starts, water flows through the internal cooling water system. To help
increase the temperature of the water, the TCS null calibration block applies a current to the
bending magnet and focus coils. Usually, the internal cooling water temperature is set to 30°C.
As distilled water flows through the internal water system, a temperature sensor diode in the
supply manifold measures the water temperature. The forward voltage decrease of the
temperature sensor diode changes at the rate of 2 mV/°C. The system reads the sensor output
continuously and sends it to the TCS where it is displayed as i223.
Signals derived from the voltage of the temperature sensor diode open or close the three-way
valve in the external cooling water system to keep the necessary temperature. The three-way
valve controls the flow of water through the heat exchanger, which controls the temperature of
the water in the internal water system to ±1°C.
If the water temperature is more than 42°C, a high temperature switch on the supply manifold
opens to disconnect the electrical supply. This switch is part of the interlock chain that includes
the low water pressure and water pump temperature.
4.2.3.8 Description of the water temperature control for linear accelerators 152178 to
152971
When the linear accelerator starts, the distilled water that flows through the internal cooling
water system increases in temperature. To help increase the temperature, the TCS null calibration
block applies a current to the bending magnet and focus coils.
As distilled water flows through the internal cooling water system, a temperature sensor diode in
the supply manifold measures the water temperature. The forward voltage decrease of the
temperature sensor diode changes at the rate of 2 mV/°C. The output of the sensor is read
continuously and sent to the treatment control system (TCS) where it is displayed as i223.
Usually, the internal cooling water temperature is set to 28 °C. The linear accelerator uses a
temperature controller on the cooling water unit to control the temperature of the distilled water.
The controller uses a K-type thermocouple on the supply manifold to measure the temperature of
the internal cooling water. To adjust the temperature, it operates an actuator attached to a three-
way valve that controls the flow of cool water from the external water system through the heat
exchanger.
If the water temperature increases to 35 °C, a high temperature software interlock operates.
If the water temperature is more than 42 °C, a high temperature switch on the supply manifold
opens and disconnects the electrical supply. This switch is part of the interlock chain that includes
the switches for the low water pressure and the water pump temperature.

Product description
Description of the dielectric gas system
4.2.3.9 Description of the water temperature control for linear accelerators from
152972
When the linear accelerator starts, the null calibration block of the TCS applies an electrical
current to the bending magnet and focus coils. This increases the water temperature to
approximately 28 °C for usual operation.
The linear accelerator uses a temperature controller on the cooling water unit to control the
temperature of the distilled water. The controller uses a K-type thermocouple on the supply
manifold to measure the temperature of the internal cooling water. To adjust the temperature, it
operates an actuator attached to a three-way valve that controls the flow of cool water through
the heat exchanger.
The supply manifold does not have a temperature sensor diode installed. The temperature
controller supplies a voltage output that is related to the temperature of the internal cooling
water. The TCS monitors this voltage from CH60 on SCC-HTB PCB 16V through AI12-B PCB 16U.
The TCS reads the output continuously and displays it on the TCS as i223. The TCS continues to
control the high temperature inhibit at 35 °C.
If the water temperature is more than 42 °C, a high temperature switch on the supply manifold
opens and disconnects the electrical supply. This switch is part of the interlock chain that includes
the switches for the low water pressure and the water pump temperature.
Note: There is no temperature switch on the pump in the cooling unit water assemblies installed on
linear accelerators after serial number 154222 and on machines which have the FRU 1519522
applied.
4.3 Description of the dielectric gas system
The dielectric gas prevents HT discharge when the RF travels through the parts of the rectangular
waveguide that do not have a vacuum.
When the linear accelerator starts, the TCS opens a top-up valve for approximately two minutes.
This lets dielectric gas from the dielectric gas cylinder fill the gas system. A pressure switch on the
cable reeling post or the RIC closes the valve automatically when the pressure is more than
1.0 bar.
A manual valve is installed in line with the supply from the gas regulator. During maintenance
tasks, the manual valve can be closed to isolate the gas cylinder. You can close the valve to
prevent the automatic operation of the gas supply when the machine state changes to Closed.
4.3.1 Description of the dielectric gas sulfur hexafluoride
Sulfur hexafluoride (SF ) is a greenhouse gas. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change
6
(IPCC) identified SF gas as the most dangerous greenhouse gas. During a 100 year period, it is
6
possible that SF gas can warm the environment 22,200 times more than CO .
6 2
You must collect and discard the SF gas correctly. It is the responsibility of the user to minimize
6
the effects on the environment as follows:
• Repair SF gas leaks promptly.
6
• Obey the local regulations to collect and discard the unwanted SF gas.
6

Product description
Description of the dielectric gas system
4.3.2 Description of the preferential breakdown (disassociation) of the
dielectric gas sulfur hexafluoride
The material that absorbs power in the high power RF load of the digital accelerator can be
damaged. If this occurs, it can cause preferential breakdown (disassociation) of the dielectric gas
in the rectangular waveguide.
Disassociation occurs in water vapor or oxygen. It is then possible to make sulfur decafluoride
(S F ) but only when there are applicable percentages of oxygen and SF .
2 10 6
The products of disassociation in water vapor and oxygen are as follows:
• Thionyl fluoride (SOF )
2
• Sulfuryl fluoride (SO F )
2 2
• Thionyl tetrafluoride (SOF ).
4
More chemical reactions can occur in water vapor. These types of chemical reaction makes weak
concentrations of hydrochloric and hydrofluoric acids.
Do not smoke cigarettes near the SF gas, because it can cause disassociation of the SF
6 6
molecules. This type of disassociation can cause a toxic mixture of gases in the air that have a
strong smell. If this occurs, go out of the room immediately, and use a pump to evacuate the
room until the room does not smell.
4.3.3 Description of the dielectric gas cylinder for linear accelerators to
152783
The dielectric gas cylinder holds the dielectric gas for the waveguide system on the linear
accelerator. The gas cylinder is installed in the cable reeling post (AREA 60), or installed on a wall
near the cable reeling post and held in position by brackets and chain straps.
Note: A gas cylinder with a maximum size of 1460 mm × 230 mm can be installed in the cable reeling
post.
A multistage gas regulator is installed to the outlet of the gas cylinder. An inlet connection kit,
supplied with the linear accelerator, can connect the gas regulator to different gas cylinder outlets
in different countries. The inlet pressure is 4.83 bar (70 psi) and the preset outlet pressure is
0.8 ±0.05 bar (12 ±0.75 psi).
4.3.4 Description of the dielectric gas cylinder for linear accelerators from
152784
The dielectric gas cylinder holds the dielectric gas for the waveguide system on the linear
accelerator. The cylinder is installed on a wall near the RIC and held in position by brackets and
chain straps.
A multistage gas regulator is installed to the outlet of the gas cylinder. An inlet connection kit,
supplied with the linear accelerator, can connect the gas regulator to different gas cylinder outlets
in different countries. The inlet pressure is 4.83 bar (70 psi) and the preset outlet pressure is
0.8 ±0.05 bar(12 ±0.75 psi).

Product description
Description of the dielectric gas system
4.3.5 Description of the dielectric gas top-up valve
The top-up valve opens and closes at regular intervals to keep the correct quantity of dielectric
gas in the rectangular waveguide. It also isolates the dielectric gas cylinder from the waveguide
when the digital accelerator is off during the system start cycle.
The top-up valve connects to the dielectric gas cylinder and the rectangular waveguide. For digital
accelerators to serial number 152783, the valve is in the cable reeling post (AREA 60). For digital
accelerators from serial number 152784, the valve is in the RIC.
When software item i336 (top-up gas) is set to 1 in Service Mode, the treatment control system
(TCS) automatically controls the operation of the top-up valve. The TCS opens the top-up valve
when the machine state changes to Closed. A pressure switch closes the top-up valve
automatically if the gas pressure increases to more than 1 bar.
4.3.6 Description of the pressure indication for the dielectric gas system
The pressure gauge shows the current gas pressure and the minimum permitted gas pressure in
the waveguide. The pressure release valve prevents the buildup of too much pressure in the
waveguide which can cause damage to the waveguide. The pressure switch stops the radiation
delivery if the waveguide pressure is not in the defined limits for safe operation.
The pressure gauge and pressure switch assembly, and the pressure release valve are installed in
AREA 22 of the gantry. The pressure release valve is installed adjacent to pressure gauge and
pressure switch assembly on the gantry drum, between the gantry drum and the modulator
assembly.
The pressure gauge measures the gas pressure in bar. The black pointer shows the gas pressure in
the waveguide, which is approximately 0.8 bar. The red pointer is set to the minimum permitted
pressure, which is usually 0.7 bar. This sets the operation points of the pressure switch.
The pressure switch is connected to the TCS as software item i243 (gas pressure). The connection
is through the IRC-B PCB on the gantry drum (AREA 14), and DIE-HTB PCB in the HTCA (AREA 16).
The TCS monitors i243 (gas pressure) and stops radiation if the switch opens.
The control knob on the pressure release valve sets the pressure at which the valve operates. A
center screw locks the control knob at a pressure setting of 1.5 bar (21.8 psi).
4.3.7 Description of the dielectric gas extraction pump
The dielectric gas extraction pump installed on the gantry drum (AREA 15) is a vacuum pump.
Before maintenance, use the dielectric gas extraction pump to evacuate the air, water vapor, and
dielectric gas from the waveguide system.
4.3.8 Operation of the control circuit of the dielectric gas system
When the digital accelerator is energized, the TCS sends i336 (top-up gas) to the ROC-ICA PCB.
This energizes relay RL7 on the IRC-C PCB. This completes the interlock circuit from interlock
transformer T2 to the coil of the dielectric gas valve (SW60A) on the cable reeling post or RIC. At
the same time, the TCS starts a 15 minute timeout counter to let the gas pressure increase and
operate the pressure switch (SW60B), which closes the valve (SW60A). If the gas pressure is too
low to operate the pressure switch (SW60B) in the timeout period, the TCS de-energizes the valve
to isolate the gas cylinder. The cause can be an empty gas cylinder or a gas leak.
The pressure gauge (AREA 22) also monitors the gas pressure. It has a minimum level switch that
is usually set to operate at 0.7 bar. The HT cannot be switched on until the pressure of the
dielectric gas increases to the necessary minimum pressure. This is because the FPLA on the DIE-
HTB PCB that monitors the pressure gauge switch is part of the HT/PRF interlock chain.

If during usual operation of the digital accelerator the gas pressure decreases to less than 0.7 bar,
the pressure gauge switch (AREA 22) opens. This sets the input voltage to the FPLA on the DIE-
HTB PCB to 0 V to stop the PRF and radiation is terminated.
4.4 Vacuum system
4.4.1 Description of the prerequisites to keep the vacuum system clean
The surfaces of the vacuum system must be clean to decrease the effect of outgassing.
Outgassing can occur because of contamination in the waveguide which causes a chemical
reaction with the waveguide material and releases absorbed gas. Almost all liquids and vapors
can prevent a high vacuum because of outgassing. If the vacuum parts are not clean, more
outgassing can occur. This has an effect on the time to get a specified vacuum or on the rate at
which you get a satisfactory vacuum pressure.
You must make sure that you:
• Touch the surfaces of clean vacuum parts with clean tools only.
• Use clean, powder-free, disposable gloves (vinyl, latex or nylon gloves) when handling
all vacuum related parts.
• Remove the dirt with a lint-free cloth.
• Remove finger grease contamination with an approved solvent.
• Do not use any grease when fitting the o-ring.
When carrying out a filament exchange like a complete gun assembly or a cathode carrier, make
sure you:
• Clean any existing vacuum surfaces of grease. For example, o-ring contact area, inner
areas of gun envelope and shunt ring.
• Use a good degreasing solvent (for example, Hypersolve and Acetone) which is locally
available and make sure to adhere to manufacturer's instructions.
Note: Elekta recommends you do not use most of the usual solvents (includes alcohol). If you use them,
it can cause long outgassing times and unsatisfactory vacuum pressure. Do not use an approved
solvent unless it is necessary.
• Use lint-free cloth to remove any grease from the surface and make sure not to get any
solvent into the waveguide.
Make sure that you keep the clean vacuum parts in an applicable container until you use them for
repair. This prevents contamination of the parts. For example, you can use a:
• Bell jar
• Desiccator
• Sealed container flushed with dry nitrogen.
Note: If the vacuum system is open to the air for more than five minutes, dirt and water vapor can get
in. Therefore, you must seal the openings of the vacuum system as quickly as it is possible.
Post the gun assembly replacement, for any subsequent filament changes involving a cathode
carrier (1007897) replacement, make sure a Viton o-ring (261308030168) is used instead of the
Nitrile o-ring that is supplied with the current spares kit.

Vacuum system
Note: For Iran, Syria, Sudan, North Korea, Cuba, and the Crimea region of Ukraine, a Nitrile o-ring must
still be used for the cathode carrier change.
4.4.2 Description of the linear accelerator parts that have a vacuum
These parts of the linear accelerator have vacuum:
• Linear accelerator waveguide (AREA 20)
• Electron gun (AREA 21)
• Input mode transformer (AREA 20, first cell)
• Output mode transformer (AREA 20, last cell)
• Input waveguide window (AREA 20)
• Output waveguide window (AREA 20)
• Flight tube assembly (AREA 24)
• Target/window assembly (AREA 24).
A high vacuum also prevents the degradation of the gas in the waveguide at the high RF power
levels used. It is necessary to keep parts of the linear accelerator at a high vacuum to get:
• Electron emission from the gun
• Free flow of electrons down the waveguide.
4.4.3 Description of the vacuum pumps that evacuate the waveguide
There are two types of pumps on the linear accelerator used to evacuate the waveguide:
• An external vacuum pump (roughing pump)
• The ion pumps in the vacuum system.
The external vacuum pump evacuates the system from atmospheric pressure until the vacuum is
better than 10-4 mbar. When the vacuum is better than 10-4 mbar, the ion pumps are switched on
and the external vacuum pump is disconnected from the digital accelerator.
Note: Only switch on the ion pumps when the vacuum is better than 10-4 mbar.
There are two types of external vacuum pumps used to evacuate the waveguide:
• A rotary vane pump
• A turbomolecular pump.
For machines with Varian ion pumps, Elekta recommends that you use the turbomolecular pump,
because it can make a better vacuum (see Figure 4.8).

When the digital accelerator operates in usual conditions, the pressure is less than 5 × 10-6 mbar.
When the digital accelerator does not operate, the standby pressure of the vacuum system is
lower than 10-6 mbar.
4.4.4 Description of the ion pumps of the linear accelerator
There are two ion pumps on the linear accelerator, which keep the pressure in the vacuum system
at less than 10-5 mbar. One ion pump is installed at the gun end of the waveguide (AREA 20) and
the second ion pump is installed at the target end.
Linear accelerators to serial number 151551 have a BOC Edwards vacuum system. There are two
ion pumps installed each with a capacity of 30 L/s. The ion pumps and their monitor circuits are
the same.
Linear accelerators from serial number 151552 have a Varian vacuum system. There are two ion
pumps installed each with a capacity of 35 L/s. The ion pumps and their monitor circuits are the
same.
4.4.5 Description of the PSUs of the Edwards ion pumps for linear
accelerators to 151551
The Edwards type EP1/30S PSUs supply the DC power for the ion pumps at the gun and target
ends of the waveguide. Each of the two ion pumps has a dedicated PSU installed in AREA 18 of
the gantry. The PSUs have protection from overload and high tension (HT) discharge. The
protection circuit removes the input supply for 30 seconds when HT discharge occurs.
The PSUs supply the ion pumps with the voltages in Table 4.1.

The PSUs get their supply from a customer supplied single-phase mains power supply and not

The front panel of each ion pump PSU has:
• Single-phase mains power supply socket
• High voltage power outlet to the ion pump
• A lamp with a cycle symbol that is on when the electrical power supply from CB10
(vacuum) is available
• EHT ON lamp that is on when the HT output is available
• Fuse F1, 4 A at 250 VAC, time delay
• Fuse F2, 250 mA at 250 VAC, no time delay.
The front panel also includes a three pin terminal block (TS2) where you can measure the current
that the PSU uses. A high current measurement across pins 2 and 3 (with a digital voltmeter set to
mA range) shows an unsatisfactory vacuum.
4.4.6 Description and operation of the vacuum control system for linear
accelerators to 151551
The vacuum control circuits of the gun and target ion pumps are the same. This is a description of
the control circuit of the target ion pump.
An HT cable from the PSU supplies the DC voltage for the target ion pump. The current that flows
in the target ion pump is in proportion to the pressure in the vacuum system. A resistor in the
current path develops an analog voltage that is in proportion to the vacuum. This voltage goes to
the SCC-HTA PCB as VAC MON T, where a Zener calibration circuit changes it to a logarithmic
scale. The SCC-HTA PCB supplies this voltage to the AI12-A PCB and the DIE-HTA PCB. The AI12-A
PCB changes this analog signal to a 12-bit digital output of vacuum pressure.
The TCS receives the digital value of vacuum pressure from the AI12-A PCB as i228 (Vac Targ). The
vacuum value displayed is the exponent to base 10 from the graph.
For example:
• Vacuum (mbar) = 2 * 10-6 = 10-5.7
• Display: i228 Vac Targ = -5.7

The DIE-HTA PCB is part of the PRF and HT ENABLE interlock chain. The DIE-HTA PCB compares
the vacuum signal from the SCC-HTA PCB with a reference voltage equivalent to a vacuum of
10-5 mbar. When the vacuum increases to more than 10-5 mbar, the DIE-HTA PCB operates hard
and soft interlocks to break the PRF ENABLE interlock chain. This causes an inhibit to stop the
output during this period and i241 appears on the TCS display. When the vacuum decreases to
less than 10-5 mbar, usual operation is restored automatically.
There is a second inhibit if the vacuum increases to more than 10-4 mbar. The DIE-HTA PCB
compares the vacuum signal with a reference voltage equivalent to a vacuum of 10-4 mbar. When
the vacuum increases to more than 10-4 mbar, the DIE-A PCB operates hard and soft interlocks.
These interlocks break the HT ENABLE chain and switch off the electrical supply to prevent
damage to the gun filament. This terminates the delivery of radiation and inhibit i239 appears on
the TCS display.
If there is a mains electrical supply failure, relay RLA in the ion pump PSU de-energizes to prevent
an incorrect indication of a high vacuum. When the relay de-energizes, it supplies a -15 VDC
voltage to operate the vacuum 10-4 mbar trip signal. This causes inhibit i239 to appear on the TCS
display.
4.4.7 Description of the Varian / Agilent ion pump control unit for linear
accelerators from 151552
The Varian / Agilent IPCU supplies electrical power to the ion pumps at the gun and target ends of
the waveguide. The IPCU is the Varian / Agilent IPCU type 529-5001-Rxxx (where xxx is the release
number, for example, 001) and contains two PSUs. The gun PSU has a maximum output power of
40 W and the target PSU has a maximum output power of 80 W. The Varian / Agilent IPCU is
installed in AREA 18.
The client interface supplies a single-phase mains power supply to the IPCU. Continuous
operation of the ion pumps is therefore possible when the linear accelerator is switched off.
Circuit breaker CB10 (vacuum) energizes the IPCU from the client supply.
There are two modes of operation in the IPCU, start and protect. To start the ion pump, the IPCU
must be in the start mode where it supplies the usual current and voltage to the pump. In protect

4.4.10 Description and operation of the vacuum control system for the gun
ion pump for linear accelerators from 151552
The 5.0 kVDC voltage from the ion pump control unit (IPCU) supplies the ion pump through an HT
cable. A logarithmic signal from the recorder output of the IPCU goes to the SCC-HTB PCB as VAC
MON G. This signal is in proportion to the current that flows in the ion pump and the current is in
proportion to the vacuum pressure. The SCC-HTB PCB filters and increases the level of the signal.
The signal then goes to the AI12-B PCB and the DIE-HTB PCB. The AI12-B PCB changes the signal
to a 12-bit digital output of vacuum pressure. The TCS receives this digital signal as i227 (Vac
Gun). The vacuum value is displayed digitally.
The DIE-HTB PCB is part of the PRF and HT ENABLE interlock chain. The DIE-HTB PCB compares
the vacuum signal from the SCC-HTB PCB with a reference voltage equivalent to a vacuum of
10-5 mbar. When the vacuum increases to more than 10-5 mbar, the DIE-HTB PCB operates hard
and soft interlocks to break the PRF ENABLE interlock chain. This causes an inhibit to stop the
output during this period and i240 10-5 Trp G appears on the TCS display. When the vacuum
decreases to less than 10-5 mbar, usual operation is restored automatically
There is a second inhibit if the vacuum increases to more than 10-4 mbar. The DIE-HTB PCB
compares the vacuum signal with a reference voltage equivalent to a vacuum of 10-4 mbar. When
the vacuum increases to more than 10-4 mbar, the DIE-B PCB operates hard and soft interlocks.
These interlocks break the HT ENABLE chain and switch off the supply to the electron gun
filament and terminates the delivery of radiation to prevent damage to the gun filament. Inhibit
i238 10-4 Trp G appears on the TCS display.
If the IPCU interlock chain breaks, the IPCU shuts down and removes the HT supply to the ion
pump. Inhibit i238 10-4 Trp G appears on the TCS display.
If there is a mains power supply failure, a failure relay in the IPCU de-energizes to prevent an
incorrect indication of a high vacuum. When the relay de-energizes, it supplies a +5.6 VDC
monitor signal to the SCC-HTB VAC MON G input. The +5.6 VDC is from the -15 VDC converter
assembly that converts -15 VDC from the HTCA in AREA 16 to +5.6 VDC. This voltage operates a
vacuum 10-4 mbar trip signal and causes inhibit i238 10-4 Trp G to appear on the TCS display.
4.4.11 Description and operation of the vacuum control system for the
target ion pump for linear accelerators from 151552
The 5.0 kVDC voltage from the ion pump control unit (IPCU) supplies the ion pump through an HT
cable. A logarithmic signal from the recorder output of the IPCU goes to the SCC-HTA PCB as VAC
MON T. This signal is in proportion to the current that flows in the ion pump and the current is in
proportion to the vacuum pressure. The SCC-HTA PCB filters and increases the level of the signal.
The signal then goes to the AI12-A PCB and the DIE-HTA PCB. The AI12-A PCB changes the signal
to a 12-bit digital output of vacuum pressure. The TCS receives this digital signal as i228 (Vac
Targ). The vacuum value is displayed digitally.
The DIE-HTA PCB is part of the PRF and HT ENABLE interlock chain. The DIE-HTA PCB compares
the vacuum signal from the SCC-HTA PCB with a reference voltage equivalent to a vacuum of

A hand operated nut opens and closes the isolation valve. A shaft connected to the nut presses an
O-ring seal against an internal shelf when the valve is closed. When you open the valve, the shaft
and O-ring move away from the shelf.
The hand operated nut operates an internal bellows around the shaft. The bellows stop the
leakage of air around the shaft when the valve is open and there is a vacuum in the waveguide.
Use the isolation valve during maintenance to isolate the waveguide when it is necessary to
replace the gun ion pump or roughing pump. This prevents possible contamination of the

5.1.4 Repairing the leaks from the water filter bowls or bleed screw
Prerequisites
• Plastic ring wrench for the filter bowl
If a filter bowl or bleed screw is too tight, the seals can twist and cause a leak of cooling water.
1 Loosely tighten the filter bowl by hand.
2 Use the plastic ring wrench to fully tighten the filter bowl.
3 If the leak continues, loosen the filter bowl by 1/8th of a turn.
4 If the leak continues, replace the seals or bleed screws as necessary.

5.1.8 Correcting an i196 pressure switch - low fault
Prerequisites
• Digital pressure gauge that measures 0.3 to 0.7 bar in units of 0.005 bar (5 to 10 psi in
units of 0.1 psi).
• Foot pump with a pressure gauge.
The water pump on the cooling water unit does not operate because of low cooling water
pressure.
CAUTION 5.3
Do not pressurize the internal cooling water system to more than 1 bar (14.5 psi). If you ignore
this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
1 Use the table that follows to correct the fault if the TCS monitor shows an inhibit i196
pressure switch - low inhibit. This inhibit is an indication of low water pressure in the
internal cooling water system.
If Then
The i196 pressure 1 Drain the internal cooling water system.
switch - low inhibit
2 Remove the valve cover from the end of the expansion
appears on the TCS.

3 Install the pressure gauge to the valve.
4 Make sure that the diaphragm in the expansion vessel has a
pressure of 0.5 bar (7.25 psi).
The pressure is too 1 Push in the pin on the valve and decrease the pressure of
high. the diaphragm in the expansion vessel to 0.5 bar (7.25 psi).
The pressure is too 1 Use the foot pump and increase the pressure of the
low. diaphragm in the expansion vessel to 0.5 bar (7.25 psi).
2 When the diaphragm pressure is 0.5 bar (7.25 psi), install the valve cover on the expansion
vessel.
3 Fill the internal cooling water system, until the water pressure is 0.9 bar (13 psi).
4 Bleed the internal cooling water system.
5 Monitor the pressure gauge on the cooling water unit.
6 Make sure that the water pressure does not decrease to less than 0.75 bar (11 psi).
5.1.9 Correcting an i223 Suspend fault for linear accelerators up to serial
number 152971
Prerequisites
• A digital voltmeter.
The motorized water valve operates correctly when it keeps the water temperature between
28 °C to 29 °C. It opens when the water temperature (i223) (Suspend) increases to approximately
28 °C.
If the system pre-checks find a fault in the cooling water system, the i223 (Suspend) interlock
operates the temperature switch SW41-10. This de-energizes CON A which keeps the digital
accelerator in the Closed machine state, and causes the i223 (Suspend) inhibit.

5.1.16 Correcting an equipment malfunction because of air in the internal
cooling water system
If air gets in the internal cooling water system, it can cause the water pressure to oscillate. This
can cause the CON-A contactor to switch on and off quickly. If this occurs, it can cause damage to
the water pump and water seals, and cause water surges. The water surges can cause a
malfunction of the LV PSU fuses.
1 If the water pump stops suddenly, bleed the internal cooling water system.
2 If the water pressure decreases by more than 0.41 bar (6 psi) when the water pump starts,
bleed the internal cooling water system.
3 If there is a fuse malfunction on the LV PSU, do the steps that follow.
a Bleed the internal cooling water system.
b Replace the applicable LV PSU fuses.
4 Make sure that the internal cooling water system operates correctly.
5.2 Dielectric gas system
5.2.1 Doing a test for leaks in the dielectric gas system
The leak test procedure continues for four days.
WARNING 5.3
Do not smoke near the dielectric gas sulfur hexafluoride (SF ), you can cause a mixture of toxic
6
gases in the air. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 5.4
Do not stay in the room if there is a strong toxic smell, or your nose, mouth, or eyes become
irritated. There can be preferential breakdown of the sulfur hexafluoride (SF ). If you ignore this
6
warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 5.5
Do not work on the RF system without wearing gloves. There can be preferential breakdown of
the sulfur hexafluoride (SF ). If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
6
CAUTION 5.5
Do not use silicon O-rings in the dielectric gas system because the mineral-based grease can
damage them and cause leaks. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the
equipment.

1 Make sure that the equipment room has a constant temperature during the four days.
Note: A change in temperature can have an effect on the pressure gauge measurements on the Control
Panel.
2 Make sure that the rectangular waveguide is full of dielectric gas.
Note: The dielectric gas pressure is on the pressure gauge in AREA 22 of the gantry.
3 On the treatment control system (TCS), open the Contactors service page.
4 Set i336 (Top-up gas) to close the gas valve.
Note: The valve must stay closed during the test period of four days.
5 Close the manual stop valve on the gas cylinder regulator to isolate the digital accelerator
from the gas supply.
The gas system cannot fill automatically when the digital accelerator is energized.
6 When the 4-day leak test is completed, and before you start the digital accelerator, make
sure that the gas pressure did not decrease by more than 0.1 bar (1.45 psi).
5.3 Vacuum system
5.3.1 Finding the cause of pressure changes in the vacuum system
It is necessary to use special tools and software items to find the cause of the pressure changes.
There are two primary causes of pressure changes:
• The outgassing of vapors, liquids, gases or solids that parts of the vacuum system
absorb
• The vacuum system has a hole that lets in air and moisture.
There are two software items that monitor the pressure in the vacuum system. These software
items give a continuous indication of the vacuum at the gun end and target end of the digital
accelerator waveguide. Use these software items to monitor for pressure changes:
• i227 (Vac Gun)
• i228 (Vac Targ).
There can be two types of leaks that cause the pressure changes:
• High pressure leaks (usually from a maintenance activity)
• Low pressure leaks (usually a parts failure in the vacuum system).
To search for leaks, it is necessary to use these special tools:
• Turbomolecular pump
• Propane-2-ol (Isopropanol, Isopropyl Alcohol, IPA).
If the leak is difficult to find, it can be necessary to use the optional mass spectrometer leak
detectors that follow, or equivalent:
• A helium leak detector
• A residual gas analyzer (RGA).
