5.3.2 Finding the high pressure leaks in the vacuum system
Elekta recommends that you use the turbomolecular pump on machines with the Varian ion
pump. The turbomolecular pump is a vacuum pump. High pressure leaks can decrease the
maximum speed or stop the turbomolecular pump.
A leak can occur around the vacuum assembly if the connections between the parts are not
assembled correctly. Leaks can also occur if there are damaged seals or loose bolts and nuts.
Frequently, maintenance activities are the cause of high pressure leaks.
To help find the leak, you can isolate areas of the waveguide vacuum system with the waveguide
isolation valve.

5.3.4 Using the optional special equipment to find leaks in the vacuum
system
Prerequisites
• A helium mass spectrometer leak detector or a residual gas analyzer (RGA) (a small
rugged mass spectrometer that can sense helium)
• Helium
• A small gas probe.
The leak test procedure is the local helium spray test. You attach the leak detector to the vacuum
system and an internal pump in the detector decreases the air pressure. You then use a gas probe
to apply helium as a spray at the area where you think there is a leak. The helium molecules move
through the leak and the detector collects them for analysis.
1 Connect the leak detector to the vacuum system.
2 Use the gas probe to apply the search gas to the area where you think there is a leak.
Note: If necessary, use a plastic bag and tape to keep the helium gas around a joint.
Note: Start at a high point and move to a low point because helium gas goes up and can cause an
incorrect indication.
If Then
The signal increases on the gas detector. The leak is where you put the gas probe.
The signal does not increase on the gas Move to the next test point.
detector.
5.3.5 Correcting a vacuum system pressure increase (RF window)
Prerequisites
• 45133180162 C-wrench
CAUTION 5.6
Do not let the vacuum pressure decrease continue. If you ignore this caution, you can cause
damage to the equipment.
If the vacuum system pressure increase continues, it can cause damage to the parts of the
waveguide. You must therefore quickly correct the problem.
If the cause is a contamination of the vacuum system, there is a worse pressure increase when
the temperature increases.
If the cause is unsatisfactory RF contact between the parts, a possible cause is:
• A loose joint
• A joint that is not aligned correctly
1 Use the C-wrench and tighten the waveguide joints.
2 Open the RF joint and check if you can hear the sound of a leak.
3 Look for a group of small black marks at the RF joint.
A group of small black marks is a sign of HT discharge.

5.3.6 Correcting an i238 or i239 10-4 inhibit fault
The ion pump control unit (IPCU) is part of an HT cable interlock chain. If the interlock chain
breaks, it causes the IPCU to shut down, which stops the power supply to the ion pumps. This
causes the i238 or i239 inhibit to appear on the TCS display. The LEDs on the front panel of the
IPCU can also flash to show there is a fault condition.
Note: The inhibit i238 is 10-4 TRP G. The inhibit i239 is 10-4 TRP T.
WARNING 5.6
Do not override the GUN and TARGET HT ground cables. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 5.7
Do not operate the system in clinical mode with an HT override. If you ignore this warning, you
can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 5.8
Do not work on the ion pump control unit (IPCU) until you switch off the electrical supply to the
unit. High voltage power is dangerous. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.

5.3.7 Correcting an LED fault indication on the ion pump control unit
When the IPCU operates correctly, the GUN and TARGET LEDs on the front panel of the IPCU do
not flash. If the IPCU has a fault condition, the GUN and TARGET LEDs flash at 4 second intervals.
1 Use the table that follows to do the troubleshooting to correct an IPCU fault if the GUN or
TARGET LEDs flash at 4 second intervals.
If Then
LED does 1 flash. 1 Do a check of the connection of the interlock HT cable.
2 Do a check for damage to the interlock HT cable.
LED does 2 flashes. 1 Do a check of the remote interlock system.
LED does 3 flashes. 1 HT overvoltage.
This fault does not switch off the HT.
LED does 4 flashes. 1 Do a check of the ADC.
LED does 5 flashes. 1 An overload of current on the HT (Short circuit).
LED does 6 flashes. 1 Make sure that the LOCAL/REMOTE switch did not move
with HT on.
2 Do a check of the LOCAL/REMOTE switch.
LED does 7 flashes. 1 The temperature in the IPCU is too high.
LED does 8 flashes. 1 Current overload is in protect mode (8 mA for 40 W
channel and 16 mA for 80 W channel).
LED does 9 flashes. 1 HT undervoltage.
5.3.8 Resetting an IPCU LED error condition in LOCAL-REMOTE I/O mode
1 Make sure the IPCU mode switch on the front panel is set to to LOCAL-REMOTE I/O.
2 On the front panel of the IPCU, set the GUN or TARGET channel switch to OFF (O) and then
switch to ON (I).

5.3.9 Resetting an IPCU fault in SERIAL mode
Prerequisites
• Computer (Laptop)
• 9-pin RS232 serial cable
• Serial communications software.
1 On the front panel of the IPCU, set the mode switch to SERIAL.
2 Install the serial cable between the serial port on the rear panel of the IPCU to the RS232
serial port on the computer.
3 On the computer, start the serial communications software.
On some Windows® operating systems you can use Hyperterminal.
4 Set the serial communications parameters to:
– COM1
– 9600 baud
– No parity
– data bits
– 1 stop bit.
5 Send the F01 command from the computer to the IPCU to reset HT channel 1.
The IPCU sends back a + symbol if it accepts the command or a - symbol if it does not
accept the command.
6 Send the F02 command from the computer to the IPCU to reset HT channel 2.
The IPCU sends back a + symbol if it accepts the command or a - symbol if it does not
accept the command.
5.3.10 Starting the IPCU after a power failure
The IPCU has an automatic start function when a power failure occurs. If the TARGET and GUN
switches are on when the power supply becomes live, the IPCU automatically switches on the HT
power.
1 Make sure that the IPCU starts automatically.
2 If the IPCU does not start, do a check of the vacuum system pressure.
Note: If the power failure was longer than two hours, the pressure in the vacuum system can be too
high.
If Then
The pressure is too high Use the turbomolecular pump to decrease the pressure in the
vacuum system.
The pressure is in limits Do the troubleshooting of the IPCU.

Troubleshooting
Vacuum system
5.3.11 Starting the ion pumps after the vacuum system was open to the air
WARNING 5.9
Do not work on the ion pump control unit (IPCU) until you switch off the electrical supply to the
unit. High voltage power is dangerous. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
The Varian (Agilent) ion pump control unit (IPCU) power supply has a lower output current than
the BOC Edwards power supply, which it replaces. This makes it more difficult for the ion pumps
to start if the vacuum system was open to the air for service.
1 Use the turbomolecular pump or the rotary vane pump to evacuate the vacuum system.
Note: Elekta recommends that you use the turbomolecular pump with the Varian ion pump because it
can get a lower pressure.
2 Make sure that the vacuum system has a good vacuum pressure.
3 At the interface cabinet or reeling interface cabinet (RIC), switch on circuit breaker CB10
(vacuum) for 10 seconds to start the ion pumps.
4 Switch off circuit breaker CB10 (vacuum).
5 Let the pressure decrease for five minutes.
6 Do steps 3 to 5 five times.
Note: This is the outgassing procedure to remove gas from the surfaces of the ion pump electrodes. The
ion pump electrodes release a large quantity of gas, usually water vapor, when the ion pumps
start.
7 At the interface cabinet or RIC, switch on circuit breaker CB10 (vacuum).
8 On the treatment control system (TCS), monitor the values of i227 (Vac Gun) and i228 (Vac
Targ). Make sure that the pressure decreases to less than -5.0.
Note: A pressure of less than -5.0 shows the ion pumps have started.
9 If the vacuum pressure decreases, go to step 13 .
10 If the vacuum pressure does not decrease, at the interface cabinet or RIC, switch off circuit
breaker CB10 (vacuum).
11 Use the turbomolecular pump for 15 minutes.
12 Do steps 7 to 11 again until the ion pumps start.
13 If the ion pumps start, close the roughing valve to isolate the turbomolecular pump.
14 If the ion pumps do not start, go to step 19 .
15 Monitor the pressure and temperature of the ion pumps during the first 30 minutes.
16 Make sure that the ion pumps do not become hot.
WARNING 5.10
Do not touch the ion pump during a fault condition because it can become very hot. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause injury.
17 If the ion pumps become hot, at the interface cabinet or RIC, switch off circuit breaker CB10
(vacuum).
Note: A hot ion pump (+60 °C) is an indication of a problem in the vacuum system. As the pump becomes
hot, there is a release of absorbed gas which decreases the vacuum.

18 Do a check for low pressure leaks in the vacuum system.
19 If there are no leaks, one of the ion pumps could be faulty.
20 Identify the defective ion pump.
21 Replace the defective ion pump.
5.3.12 Guidelines to troubleshoot the vacuum system
The following are some of the guidelines to troubleshoot the vacuum system.
• History of the equipment
– Check the history of the equipment using QlikSense Alarms and Diagnostics
application. If you do not have access to the application, check with Tier 3
support group for Intellimax vacuum trending data.
– Use the available data to investigate CLM cases, changed parts and recent site
visits on dates surrounding the vacuum changes.
• Initial checks
– Check that the gun pump is powered by gun pump supply and the target pump is
powered by target pump supply.
– Check the offset value. The part 10 value of items 227 and 228 must be:
Table 5.6 Correct offset values
Edwards pump -7100
Varian pump -7650
– If vacuum value is better than -6.94, then check the history as the value is
unlikely to be correct, and could indicate a hardware problem.
• Important housekeeping
– Ensure good vacuum practice is used throughout.
– Wear gloves.
– Do not use grease on o-rings
– Vent vacuum system with nitrogen. Use the Vacuum Purge and Vent Tool Kit
(Product Code/Movex Part Number : 1020015)
– Do not re-use seals.

Prerequisites

Do not use any other water type other than distilled water in the water cooling system. Other

6.1.8 Calibrating the water pressure monitor system
The water pressure in the internal water system must be constant. If the water pressure gauge
does not show a constant pressure, there is air in the internal cooling water system. This can have
an effect on the correct pressure switch setup.
Use software items i196 (P. Switch Low) and i197 (P. Switch High) to calibrate the water
temperature.
1 On the TCS, open Override Machine Item dialog box.
2 Remove the cover of the housing of the pressure monitor switch (1).
1
NOT TO SCALE 004003 ©2012 Elekta Limited
Figure 6.26 Housing of pressure monitor switch
3 Set the water pressure to 7.5 psi ±0.25 (approximately 0.52 bar).
4 Adjust the low pressure switch (LAY 562) until the i196 inhibit appears (water pressure low).
5 Increase the water pressure to 15 psi ±0.25 (approximately 1.03 bar).
6 Make sure that the i196 inhibit clears.
7 Adjust the high pressure switch (LAY 561) until the i197 inhibit appears (water pressure
high).
8 Decrease the water pressure to 10 psi ±0.25 (approximately 0.69 bar).
9 Make sure that the i197 inhibit clears.
10 Do steps 3 to 9 again until the pressure switch settings are correct.
11 Decrease the water pressure to less than 5 psi (approximately 0.35 bar) and make sure that
CON-A is de-energized.
12 Increase the water pressure to 12 psi ±0.25 (approximately 0.83 bar).
13 If it is necessary, bleed the air from the cooling water system.
14 Make sure that the water pressure is stable at 12 psi ±0.25 (approximately 0.83 bar).
15 Install the cover on the housing of the pressure monitor switch.
6.1.9 Doing a test of the water valve calibration
The water valve helps to control the water temperature in the internal cooling water system. If
the water valve does not operate correctly, the water temperature can be too low or too high.

Use software items i132 (Wtr Valve) and i223 (Water Temp) to do a test of the water valve
calibration.
1 On the TCS, open the Modulator service page.
2 Open the Edit Machine Item Part dialog box.
3 Record the value of i223 part 10.
4 Increase the value of i223 part 10 until the displayed temperature is more than 30.0 °C.
5 Make sure that the water valve moves to the open position.
6 Make sure that the actual value of i132 changes.
7 Decrease the value of i223 part 10 until the displayed temperature is less than 30.0 °C.
8 Makes sure that the water valve moves to the closed position.
9 Make sure that the actual value of i132 changes.
10 Adjust the value of i223 part 10 until the temperature is 30.0 °C.
11 Make sure that the water valve moves to the center position.
12 Make sure that the Actual value of i132 gradually changes across the full range of
movement, and does not go through zero.
13 Adjust the value of i223 part 10 to close the water valve.

6.1.10 Doing a test of the water flow interlock

system. If the water flow decreases below a specified level, the water flow interlock stops the
linear accelerator.

Figure 6.27).

It is necessary to add the MC1+ inhibitor to the internal cooling water system if you:
• Flush the system with EDTA.
• Replace a copper part, for example, the bending magnet coils.
• Do a procedure where water is drained from any part of the water system, for
example, copper parts, and when switches are replaced. New sealed distilled water
and MC1+ must be added.

CAUTION 6.7
Do not use any other water type other than distilled water in the water cooling system. Other
water types include mains tap water, demineralized, deionized, reverse-osmosis (RO), or been
through any other similar process. These water types can remove metal elements, usually
oxides, from the system pipes and metal enclosures, which can cause blockages in the system
pipes. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.

6.3.4 Filling the internal cooling water system

oxides, from the system pipes and metal enclosures, which can cause blockages in the system
pipes. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.

Prerequisites

to zero).
10 Close the bleed valve on the water supply manifold.
11 Do steps 8 to 10 again until there are no air bubbles in the water flow from the supply
manifold.
12 Let the water pressure increase to a minimum of 0.75 bar (11 psi).
13 Quickly open the bleed valve on the water return manifold (to release the water pressure to
zero).
14 Make sure that there are no air bubbles in the water flow from the return manifold.
15 Close the bleed valve on the water return manifold.
16 Let the water pressure increase to a minimum of 0.75 bar (11 psi).
17 Bleed the filter bowl, RF load, magnetron and water pump at the bleed valves until there is
no air in the system.
18 Fill the internal cooling water circuit again.

6.3.6 Replacing the cooling unit water pump
Prerequisites
• Two hoses approximately 2 m long, internal diameter 22 mm
• Water container, 10 L capacity
• Lifting equipment (hoist, lifting strap or sling). The water pump weighs approximately
20 kg.
Note: Before you proceed with the water pump replacement procedure, verify if the cooling unit water
pump kit upgrade has been applied or not:
• If the cooling unit pump kit upgrade (FRU) has been applied (indicated by the presence
of the soft start module and absence of thermal overload switch on the water pump),
proceed with this replacement procedure.
• If the cooling unit pump kit upgrade (FRU) has not been applied, refer to the product
label on the water pump and confirm it is a Calpeda or a Lowara pump:
– If it is a Calpeda pump, proceed with the Cooling unit water pump upgrade
(Calpeda) instructions.
– If it is a Lowara pump, proceed with the Cooling unit water pump upgrade
(Lowara) instructions.

area at the interface cabinet. You can get a high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause fatal injury.

water pump.
2 Drain the internal cooling water system. See the procedure for draining the internal cooling
water system.
WARNING 6.13
Do not remove the cover of the three-phase connection box before it is cool. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause serious injury.
3 Remove the cover of the three-phase connection box on the water pump carefully.
Be careful not to damage the seal below the cover.

6.3.7 Cooling unit water pump upgrade (Calpeda)
The cooling unit water pump upgrade information include a set of procedures that allow you to
upgrade the cooling unit water pump. Elekta recommends a replacement of the water pump if:

upgrade has been applied or not:

of the soft start module and absence of thermal overload switch on the water pump),

• If the cooling unit pump kit upgrade (FRU) has not been applied, refer to the product
label on the water pump and confirm it is a Calpeda or a Lowara pump.

– If it is a Lowara pump, proceed with the Cooling unit water pump upgrade

6.3.7.8 Return to work after the water pump upgrade
Complete the following tasks to return the linear accelerator to work after the water pump
upgrade procedure:
1 Bleed the internal cooling water system.

water pressure.
5 On the control system, set the machine state to Preparatory.
6 Rotate the gantry and continue bleeding air from the water filter bowls.
7 Make sure that the temperature controller switches on and that it shows the temperature.

6.3.8.8 Return to work after the water pump upgrade
Complete the following tasks to return the linear accelerator to work after the water pump
upgrade procedure:
1 Bleed the internal cooling water system.
2 On the control system, set the machine state to System On.
3 Make sure the water pump is rotating in the correct direction.
4 Once the water pump is running, bleed the air from the water filter bowls and top up the
water pressure.
5 On the control system, set the machine state to Preparatory.
6 Rotate the gantry and continue bleeding air from the water filter bowls.
7 Make sure that the temperature controller switches on and that it shows the temperature.

6.3.9 Replacing the water filters
Prerequisites
• Rubber gloves
• Safety glasses
• Water container, 10 L capacity.
There are two water filters in the internal cooling water system. You must replace them if you
think that there is contamination in the water system.
1 Rotate the gantry to 160°.
2 At the interface cabinet or the RIC, switch off the circuit breakers CB11 (water pump) and
CB7 (ICCA).
WARNING 6.35
Do not get Benzotriazole on your skin, in your eyes, or breathe the vapor. Put on the applicable
personal protective equipment. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
3 Put on the personal protective equipment.
4 Open the drain valves on the supply and return manifolds to release the pressure in the
internal water system.
5 Put a water container below the water filter to catch the water.
6 Loosen the filter bowl and make sure that you do not cause damage to the O-ring seal.
7 Disconnect the push-fit pipe.
8 Loosen the bleed screw on the filter bowl to drain the remaining water.
9 Remove the filter bowl.
10 Remove the filter element from the filter bowl and discard the element safely it contains
benzotriazole.
11 Follow local procedures to discard the filter element safely, it contains benzotriazole.
12 Clean the filter bowl.
13 Put a new 5 micron filter element into the filter bowl.
14 Install the filter bowl in its housing, and make sure that you do not cause damage to the O-
ring seal.
15 Remove the water container.
16 Follow the local procedures to discard the drained water safely, it contains benzotriazole.
17 Fill the internal cooling water circuit.
18 Bleed the internal cooling water circuit.
Related Links:
Filling the internal cooling water system on page 161
Bleeding the internal cooling water system on page 165

6.3.12 Replacing the flow switches
The flow switches on the return manifold monitor the cooling water flow to the magnetron and
the target.
1 Rotate the gantry to get access to the cooling water unit.
2 Drain the internal cooling water system.
3 Record the wire connections on TS41A.
4 Disconnect the wires from TS41A.
5 Remove the flow switch from the return manifold.
6 Install the new flow switch in the return manifold.
7 Connect the wires to the recorded connections on TS41A.
8 Fill the internal cooling water system.
9 Bleed the internal cooling water system.
10 On the treatment control system (TCS), set the machine state to System on, and then to
Closed or Preparatory.
11 Make sure that the temperature controller switches on and that it displays a temperature
on the top and bottom LCD displays.

LCDs is less than 1 °C.
13 If the difference is more than 1 °C, make sure that the three-way valve operates between
0% and 100%.
14 If the difference stays at more than 1 °C, calibrate the temperature controller.

6.3.13 Replacing the push-fit connectors
Prerequisites
Plastic pipe cutter.
1 Drain the internal cooling water system.
2 Push and hold the release button on the push-fit connector and remove the pipe.
3 Remove the push-fit connector.
4 Use the pipe cutter to cut a minimum of 10 mm from the end of the pipe. Make sure that it
is a clean, square cut and the remaining pipe is not damaged.
Note: If there are grooves in the end of a pipe, it can cause a coolant leakage from the push-fit
connector.
5 Install the new push-fit connector.
6 Push the pipe into the push-fit connector until it engages with a click.
7 Hold the connector body and pull the pipe. It must not come out.
This makes sure that the pipe connection is correct.
8 Fill the internal cooling water system.
9 Bleed the internal cooling water system.
10 On the TCS, set the machine state to Closed.
11 Make sure that there are no leaks from the push-fit connector.

6.3.14 Replacing the temperature controller
The temperature controller monitors and sets the temperature of the internal water cooling
system. It has a liquid crystal display (LCD) that shows temperature values and fault codes.
1 Rotate the gantry to get access to the water cooling unit.
2 At the interface cabinet or RIC, switch off the circuit breakers CB11 (water pump) and CB7
(ICCA).
3 Cut, remove, and discard the cable tie from the cables of the temperature controller.
4 Disengage the sleeve clips (1) from the body of the temperature controller.
Note: Make sure that you do not put a strain on the cable loom.

13 Install the temperature controller in the bracket and the sleeve. Make sure that the clips
engage to hold the temperature controller in position.
14 Fill the internal cooling water system.
15 Bleed the internal cooling water system.
16 On the control system, set the machine state of the linear accelerator to Closed.
17 Make sure that the temperature controller switches on and shows a temperature.
18 Make sure that there are no leaks in the thermocouple housing.
19 After approximately 5 minutes, make sure that the difference between the top and bottom
LCDs of the temperature controller is less than 1 °C.
20 If the difference is more than 1 °C, make sure that the three-way valve operates between
0% and 100%.
21 If the difference stays at more than 1 °C, do the water temperature calibration procedure.

6.3.18 Replacing the soft start module with existing cable form
Follow this procedure if the existing cable form is in good condition:
1 Rotate the gantry to a convenient position for working on the soft start module.
2 Switch off the linear accelerator.
3 Record the positions of the cable lays connected to the soft start module and disconnect
the cable lays from the terminals of the soft start module.
4 Cut the cable tie that secures the cable form to the mounting plate.
5 Remove the two bolts that secure the mounting plate to the cooling unit frame and remove
the soft start assembly.
6 Remove the new cable form from the new soft start module.
7 Mount the new soft start module to the cooling unit frame using the original fixings.
8 Reconnect the cable lays in the positions recorded above.
9 Make sure that the 3 potentiometers on the soft start module are all turned fully counter
clockwise.
10 Switch on the linear accelerator and make sure that the pump starts without causing
Contactor A chatter.
6.3.19 Replacing the soft start module
Follow this procedure if the existing cable form is damaged:
1 Remove the 3 phase terminal cover from the motor.
2 Record the positions and disconnect the cable lays from the motor.
3 Record positions and disconnect the cable lays from TS13 A 1, 2, 3, 4 and 16.
4 Remove cable ties to free the cable form.
5 Remove the two bolts that secure the mounting plate to the cooling unit frame and remove
the soft start assembly with the old cable form.
6 Mount the new soft start module to the cooling unit frame using the original fixings and run
the cable form to the pump motor and TS13A.
7 Reconnect the cable lays in the positions recorded.
8 Make sure that the 3 potentiometers on the soft start module are all turned fully counter
clockwise.
9 Switch on the linear accelerator and make sure that the pump starts without causing
Contactor A chatter.
6.3.20 Calibrating the water temperature
1 On the control system, set the machine state of the linear accelerator to System on and
then to Closed.
2 Set the Gun Current to Standby.
Note: The machine state will not go to Preparatory, if the temperature on the TCS is less than 10 °C.
3 On the control system, open the Modulator service page.
4 Make sure that the temperature value displayed is positive.
5 If the temperature is a negative value, on the rear face of the gantry, invert the temperature
diode connections.

6 On the control system, set the machine state of the linear accelerator to Closed or
Preparatory.
7 Open the Calibration service page.
8 Type i223 (Water Temp) in Cal.item 1.
9 Type the displayed value on the temperature controller LCD in Cal.value as a 4-digit
number.
Type 2820 for a temperature of 28.2 °C.
10 Make sure that the temperature value on the display changes to the correct value.
11 Type 3500 in Limit high, this sets the high temperature limit to 35.00 (35 °C).

15 If the temperature difference is more than 1 °C, do the procedure again.
16 On the Calibration service page, click Save.
17 Select Save LINAC Calibration.

6.4.4 Filling the cooling water system
Prerequisites
• Two hoses of minimum 1 m length and 12 mm inner diameter
• Two containers, 25 L for the distilled water (one contains the distilled water, one to
catch the distilled water that comes out as you fill the system).
• 25 L (5.5 gal) of distilled water and MC1+ inhibitor mixture.
CAUTION 6.15
Do not use any other water type other than distilled water in the water cooling system. Other
water types include mains tap water, demineralized, deionized, reverse-osmosis (RO), or been
through any other similar process. These water types can remove metal elements, usually
oxides, from the system pipes and metal enclosures, which can cause blockages in the system
pipes. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
WARNING 6.38
Do not get the MC1+ inhibitor on your skin, in your eyes or breathe the vapor. It contains
Benzotriazole. Use the applicable personal protective equipment (PPE). If you ignore this
warning, you can cause serious injury.
Use the electric water pump on the RIC to fill the internal cooling water system with distilled
water mixture. When you fill the internal cooling water system, the water pressure will increase.
You can monitor the water pressure with the pressure gauge on the cooling water unit.
Note: Do not supply electrical power to the linear accelerator when you rotate the gantry.
Note: Make sure there are no leaks when you fill the internal cooling water system.
1 Rotate the gantry to 180° to get access to the cooling water unit.
2 At the RIC, switch off circuit breakers CB11 (water pump) and CB7 (ICCA).

6.4.5 Bleeding the cooling water system
Prerequisites
• Two hoses of minimum 1 m length and 12 mm inner diameter.
• Container, 10 L (to catch the distilled water that comes out as you bleed the system).
This task is applicable to linear accelerators from 152972.
CAUTION 6.16
Do not operate the system with air in the internal cooling water system. This can cause damage
to the motor of the cooling water pump, water seal, LV PSU fuses, and power supply of the XVI
system.
CAUTION 6.17
Do not use demineralized water in the cooling water system it removes metal elements, usually
oxides, from the system pipes and metal enclosures. The removed metal elements can be the
cause of blockages in the system pipes. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the
equipment.
CAUTION 6.18
Do not pressurize the internal cooling water system to more than 15 psi (1 bar). If you ignore
this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.

6.4.6 Replacing the cooling unit water pump
Prerequisites
• Two hoses approximately 2 m long, internal diameter 22 mm
• Water container, 10 L capacity
• Lifting equipment (hoist, lifting strap or sling). The water pump weighs approximately
20 kg.
Note: Before you proceed with the water pump replacement procedure, verify if the cooling unit water
pump kit upgrade has been applied or not:
• If the cooling unit pump kit upgrade (FRU) has been applied (indicated by the presence
of the soft start module and absence of thermal overload switch on the water pump),
proceed with this replacement procedure.
• If the cooling unit pump kit upgrade (FRU) has not been applied, refer to the product
label on the water pump and confirm it is a Calpeda or a Lowara pump:
– If it is a Calpeda pump, proceed with the Cooling unit water pump upgrade
(Calpeda) instructions.
– If it is a Lowara pump, proceed with the Cooling unit water pump upgrade
(Lowara) instructions.
WARNING 6.40
Do not do work on the linear accelerator before you isolate the electrical supply to the work
area at the interface cabinet. You can get a high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause fatal injury.
1 Rotate the gantry to provide a level platform and suitable access to replace the cooling
water pump.
2 Drain the internal cooling water system. See the procedure for draining the internal cooling
water system.
WARNING 6.41
Do not remove the cover of the three-phase connection box before it is cool. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause serious injury.
3 Remove the cover of the three-phase connection box on the water pump carefully.
Be careful not to damage the seal below the cover.
4 Record the location of the three-phase electrical supply wires and the ground (earth) wire.
5 Disconnect the cables (1), (2), (3) and the ground cable (4) from the three-phase connection
box.

6.4.11 Replacing the push-fit connectors
Prerequisites
Plastic pipe cutter or equivalent tool.
This task is applicable for linear accelerators from serial number 152972.
1 Drain the internal cooling water system.
2 Push and hold the release button on the push-fit connector to remove the pipe.
Note: You can only remove the pipe when you push and hold the release button.
3 Remove the unserviceable push-fit connector.
4 Use the plastic pipe cutter to remove a minimum of 10 mm from the end of the pipe. Make
sure that you make a clean square cut.
Note: This prevents possible leaks from grooves on the pipe.
5 Install the replacement push-fit connector.
6 Push the pipe into the push-fit connector until you hear a click. Pull the pipe and make sure
that it does not come out.
7 Fill the internal cooling water system.
8 Bleed the internal cooling water system.
9 Make sure that there are no leaks from the push-fit connector.

6.4.18 Replacing the soft start module with existing cable form
Follow this procedure if the existing cable form is in good condition:
1 Rotate the gantry to a convenient position for working on the soft start module.
2 Switch off the linear accelerator.
3 Record the positions of the cable lays connected to the soft start module and disconnect
the cable lays from the terminals of the soft start module.
4 Cut the cable tie that secures the cable form to the mounting plate.
5 Remove the two bolts that secure the mounting plate to the cooling unit frame and remove
the soft start assembly.
6 Remove the new cable form from the new soft start module.
7 Mount the new soft start module to the cooling unit frame using the original fixings.
8 Reconnect the cable lays in the positions recorded above.
9 Make sure that the 3 potentiometers on the soft start module are all turned fully counter
clockwise.
10 Switch on the linear accelerator and make sure that the pump starts without causing
Contactor A chatter.
6.4.19 Replacing the soft start module
Follow this procedure if the existing cable form is damaged:
1 Remove the 3 phase terminal cover from the motor.
2 Record the positions and disconnect the cable lays from the motor.
3 Record positions and disconnect the cable lays from TS13 A 1, 2, 3, 4 and 16.
4 Remove cable ties to free the cable form.
5 Remove the two bolts that secure the mounting plate to the cooling unit frame and remove
the soft start assembly with the old cable form.
6 Mount the new soft start module to the cooling unit frame using the original fixings and run
the cable form to the pump motor and TS13A.
7 Reconnect the cable lays in the positions recorded.
8 Make sure that the 3 potentiometers on the soft start module are all turned fully counter
clockwise.
9 Switch on the linear accelerator and make sure that the pump starts without causing
Contactor A chatter.

6.8 Replacing the water transfer blocks
6.8.1 Description of the water transfer block kits
There are two modification kits for the replacement of the water transfer blocks. Send a purchase
order for the correct kit for the configuration of your linear accelerator:
• 1022473, the full bending magnet kit, OR
• 1022493, the bending magnet water blocks and slotted bus bar kit.
For linear accelerators before 152531, break the vacuum to remove the bending magnet
assembly first, and then replace the water transfer blocks.
It is possible that some linear accelerators before 152531 have a completed installation of one of
these kits. If it is necessary to replace the water transfer blocks, use the procedure for linear
accelerators after 152531.
Linear accelerators after 152531 have slotted bus bars which let you replace the water transfer
blocks more easily. If it is necessary, you can send a purchase order for the water transfer blocks
independently because they are field replaceable units (FRU).

Removing unwanted material from the inner surfaces of the bending coils on page 71

6.9 Repairing the dielectric gas system
6.9.1 Preparing for the dielectric gas SF removal
6
To prepare to remove the dielectric gas, identify the contents of the personal protective
equipment (PPE) kit, the consumables, and the first aid kit. Use the applicable consumables to
complete the clean after the removal of the dielectric gas.
The risk of hydrofluoric acid (HFA) in the gas and vacuum subsystems is low, but you must follow
the safety precautions while you do the servicing work in the area. It is necessary to remove the
HFA from the gas and vacuum subsystems if there was a preferential breakdown of the dielectric
gas sulfur hexafluoride (SF ). HFA is highly corrosive and a powerful contact poison. Because of
6
the ability of HFA to penetrate human tissue, poisoning can occur easily if the HFA touches your
skin or eyes, or if you inhale or swallow it.
Table 6.15 PPE kit contents
Item Description Quantity
1 Gloves 2 pairs
2 Safety glasses 1 pair
3 Face mask 1
4 Coveralls 1
Table 6.16 Consumables
Item Description Quantity
1 Paper towels As required
2 Disposable bags As required
3 Vacuum cleaner 1
4 Isopropyl alcohol As required
Note: Get the consumables from local suppliers.
Table 6.17 First aid kit
Item Description Quantity
1 Calcium gluconate gel As applicable
Note: Get the content of the first aid kit from local suppliers.

6.10 Repairing the waveguide vacuum system
6.10.1 Filling the vacuum system with dry nitrogen using the vacuum purge
and vent tool kit
Prerequisites
• A cylinder of dry nitrogen gas (99.9% pure).
• A pressure control valve with two pressure gauges that shows the gas pressure in the
cylinder and the outlet flow.
• The vacuum purge and vent tool kit (1020015).
Elekta recommends to use this procedure to fill the vacuum system with nitrogen. This is because
this tool kit maintains a positive pressure of nitrogen in the vacuum system to keep the air out
during maintenance. This also helps to decrease the time taken to evacuate the vacuum system
with the turbomolecular pump to two hours.
1 On the TCS, set the gun filament to 0 A, and let the temperature decrease for 30 minutes.
2 At the interface cabinet or RIC, switch off the circuit breaker CB10 (vacuum).
3 Do a check of the one-way valves (1) on the purge adapter (2) to make sure they are
correctly installed (see Figure 6.157).

6.10.2 Filling the vacuum system with dry nitrogen
Prerequisites
• A cylinder of compressed, dry nitrogen gas (99.9% pure).
• A pressure control valve with two pressure gauges that shows the gas pressure in the
cylinder and the outlet flow.
Note: To fill the vacuum system with dry nitrogen, Elekta recommends to always choose the Vacuum
purge and vent tool kit method, when possible. In the absence of the tool kit, you can choose to
use this procedure.
When it is necessary to disassemble parts of the vacuum system, Elekta recommends that the
system is filled with dry nitrogen gas. This helps to prevent contamination of the vacuum system,
and decreases the outgassing time.
Do not use dry nitrogen gas from a cylinder, in which the gas pressure is less than 14 bar (200 psi).
If the pressure is less than 14 bar (200 psi), the dry nitrogen gas can contain water vapor that can
cause contamination of the vacuum system.
1 On the TCS, set the gun filament to 0 A and let the temperature decrease for 30 minutes.
2 At the interface cabinet or RIC, switch off the circuit breaker CB10 (vacuum).
3 Make sure that the pressure of the dry nitrogen gas in the gas cylinder is more than 14 bar
(200 psi).
4 Fill a large, clean bag with dry nitrogen gas from the gas cylinder.
5 Remove the protective cap from the roughing valve.

6.10.3 Replacing the ion pump on linear accelerators to serial number
151551
Linear accelerators to serial number 151551 have the BOC Edwards vacuum system. Elekta cannot
supply BOC Edwards ion pumps or power supplies. Elekta supplies kits to replace the ion pump
and the power supplies.
Each kit contains the necessary replacement instructions. See the Technical Newsletter RT353 for
more information.
1 If it is necessary to replace the ion pump, use the ion pump replacement kit.
2 If it is necessary to replace the power supply units, use the PSU replacement kit.

6.10.4 Replacing the ion pump on linear accelerators from serial number
151552
Linear accelerators from serial number 151552 have the Varian vacuum system. All parts are
available.
The ion pumps are directly connected to a different single-phase power supply, therefore, they
stay on when the linear accelerator is switched off.
WARNING 6.60
Do not do work on the equipment before you disconnect the electrical supply to the work area
to prevent a high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 6.61
Do not touch the ion pump during a fault condition because it can become very hot. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause injury.
1 At the interface cabinet, switch off the circuit breaker CB10 (vacuum).
2 If it is necessary, replace damaged HT power cables.
3 Remove the unserviceable ion pump.
4 Install the replacement ion pump.
Note: The ion pump and magnet is one replacement part.
5 At the interface cabinet, switch on the circuit breaker CB10 (vacuum).
6 Evacuate the linear accelerator waveguide.
7 Do the vacuum system test.

6.10.7 Evacuating the waveguide
6.10.7.1 Evacuating the linear accelerator waveguide with the rotary vane pump
For machines with Varian ion pumps, Elekta recommends that you use the turbomolecular pump,
because it can make a better vacuum.
Continuously monitor the rotary vane pump while it operates. If there is a power outage, close
the roughing valve immediately to prevent contamination of the waveguide by oil fumes from the
rotary vane pump.
This procedure uses software items i227 (Vac Gun) and i228 (Vac Targ).
1 At the interface cabinet or the RIC, switch off the circuit breaker CB10 (vacuum).
This removes electrical power from the ion pump PSU or ion pump controller unit (IPCU).
2 Make sure that the vacuum system contains dry nitrogen at atmospheric pressure.
3 On the rotary vane pump, do the following steps:
a Close the isolation valve near the flexible hose.
b Close the gas ballast valve.
c Make sure that the flexible hose is closed to the air with a cap or seal.
d Make sure that the oil level in the rotary vane pump is approximately 75% of
maximum volume.
e If it is necessary, add oil until the oil level is correct.
f Switch off the vacuum gauge.
4 Rotate the gantry to get access to the roughing valve (1) (see Figure 6.182).

accelerator. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
6 Connect the flexible hose from the rotary vane pump to the roughing valve (1).
7 On the linear accelerator, make sure that:
a All vacuum joints are safely connected.
b The roughing valve (1) is closed.
c The waveguide isolation valve (2) at the end of the waveguide is closed.
8 Connect the power cable of the rotary vane pump to an applicable electrical supply socket
near the gantry.
9 Start the rotary vane pump.
10 Continuously monitor the rotary vane pump while it operates. If there is a power outage,
close the roughing valve immediately.
11 Open the two isolation valves on the rotary vane pump.
12 After 60 seconds, switch on the vacuum gauge.
13 After approximately two minutes, when the flexible hose is evacuated, open the roughing
valve (1).
Initially the indication on the vacuum gauge increases, before it starts to decrease again
when the waveguide is evacuated.
14 Open the waveguide isolation valve (2).
Note: If there is a defect in the vacuum system, a leak for example, the time to evacuate the system to a
correct vacuum pressure can be more than three hours.
CAUTION 6.31
Do not start the ion pumps before the vacuum pressure is correct. If you ignore this caution,
you can cause damage to the equipment.
Not controlled when downloaded or printed
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 45133702093_11
Corrective Maintenance Manual - Cooling, Gas, and Vacuum Systems
© Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved. Page 315

Repairing the system
Repairing the waveguide vacuum system
15 Operate the rotary vane pump for three hours, or until the pressure on the vacuum gauge is
less than 6.7 × 10–3 mbar (5 × 10–3 Torr).
Note: There can be a large difference between the pressure gauge indication and the pressure in the
system because the vacuum gauge measurement point is not near the ion pumps.
16 At the interface cabinet or the RIC, switch on the circuit breaker CB10 (vacuum) when the
system vacuum pressure is less than 6.7 × 10–3 mbar (5 × 10–3 Torr).
This supplies electrical power to the ion pump PSU or ion pump controller unit (IPCU).
17 For linear accelerators before 151552 that have an Edwards BOC type EP1/30S ion pump
PSU, make sure that the two green LEDs on the PSU are on.
a Make sure that the two green LEDs on the PSU are on.
b Close the roughing valve.
18 For linear accelerators from 151552, which have an ion pump controller (IPCU), do the steps
that follow.
a Make sure that the two channel switches GUN and TARGET on the front panel of the
IPCU are set to ON/I.
b Make sure that the MODE switch is set to LOCAL/REM I/O.
c Let the rotary vane pump continue to operate.
d After 3 minutes, make sure that the two LEDs on the front panel continue to stay on.
Note: The two LEDs flash intermittently when the system starts.
19 Monitor the ion pumps continuously and make sure that their temperature does not
increase.
20 If the ion pumps gets warm to touch, do the steps that follow.
a At the interface cabinet or the RIC, switch off the circuit breaker CB10 (vacuum).
b Continue to operate the rotary vane pump for a longer period to get a better vacuum.
c At the interface cabinet or the RIC, switch on the circuit breaker CB10 (vacuum).
d Monitor the ion pumps continuously and make sure that their temperature does not
increase.
e Do steps a to d until the temperature of the ion pumps does not increase during
operation.
f If you cannot get a better vacuum, do the troubleshooting to find the leaks in the
vacuum system.
21 On the control system, open the AFC or Gun Servo page.
22 Make sure that the indication in the software items i227 and i228 is less than -4.5.
23 Make sure that the ion pumps operate satisfactorily.
24 Shut down the rotary vane pump.
Related Links:
Shutting down the rotary vane pump on page 318
6.10.7.2 Evacuating the linear accelerator waveguide with the turbomolecular pump
For machines with Varian ion pumps, Elekta recommends that you use the turbomolecular pump,
because it can make a better vacuum.

Repairing the system
Repairing the waveguide vacuum system
1 At the interface cabinet or the reeling interface cabinet (RIC), switch off the circuit breaker
CB10 (vacuum).
This removes electrical power from the ion pump PSU or ion pump controller unit (IPCU).
2 Fill the vacuum system with dry nitrogen to atmospheric pressure.
3 Rotate the gantry to get access to the roughing valve (1) (see Figure 6.183).
CAUTION 6.32
Do not rotate the gantry after you connect the turbomolecular pump assembly to the linear
accelerator. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
4 Remove the protective cap from the roughing valve (1).
5 Use the applicable adapter and connect the flexible hose of the turbomolecular pump to
the roughing valve (1).
6 On the linear accelerator, make sure that all vacuum joints are safely connected.
7 Connect the power cable of the turbomolecular pump to an applicable electrical supply
socket near the gantry.
8 Open the roughing valve (1) and the waveguide isolation valve (2).

18 On the treatment control system (TCS), open the AFC or Gun Servo page.

6.10.7.3 Shutting down the rotary vane pump
This shutdown procedure makes sure that the foreline trap of the rotary vane pump stays
evacuated. It also prevents contamination of the zeolite from moisture in the air.

Repairing the system
Repairing the waveguide vacuum system
1 Switch off the vacuum pump gauge.
2 On the digital accelerator, close the waveguide isolation valve and the roughing valve.
3 Close the isolation valves on the rotary vane pump.
4 Switch off the electrical supply to the rotary vane pump.
5 Disconnect the flexible hose from the roughing valve.
6 Install the protective caps to the roughing valve and the free end of the flexible hose.
6.10.7.4 Shutting down the turbomolecular pump
1 On the digital accelerator, close the waveguide isolation valve and the roughing valve.
2 Switch off the turbomolecular pump.
3 When the speed of the turbomolecular pump decreases to less than half of full speed, open
the release valve on the turbomolecular pump.
Note: When the release valve is open, the pressure in the turbomolecular pump and flexible hose
increases to atmospheric pressure to prevent the suction of oil vapor into the body of the pump.
4 Disconnect the flexible hose from the roughing valve.
5 Install the protective cap on the roughing valve.
6 Install the protective cap on the free end of the flexible hose.
6.10.8 Doing a test of the vacuum system on linear accelerators to serial
number 151551
Prerequisites
Multimeter
The current that flows into the ion pump is in proportion to the vacuum pressure. If the current is
too high, there is a vacuum system fault.
You must do a vacuum system test after a:
• Control electronics repair.
• Replacement of a vacuum system part.
1 Evacuate the waveguide system with the rotary vane pump or the turbomolecular pump.
2 At the interface cabinet, switch on the circuit breaker CB10 (vacuum) to start the ion
pumps.
3 Set the multimeter to the mA range.
4 At the front panel of each ion pump PSU, connect a multimeter across pins 2 and 3 of
terminal block TS2.
5 Measure the current flow of each ion pump (see Figure 6.184).
Note: The current value from each ion pump must gradually decrease to a minimum value after you
start the vacuum.

Repairing the system
Detecting gun end contamination
6.11 Detecting gun end contamination
CAUTION 6.34
Do not bring the machine to Closed state before evacuating the waveguide. If you ignore this
caution, you can cause damage to the ion pumps and the electron gun.
1 Close the isolation valve to isolate the waveguide from the gun end ion pump and
evacuation port.
2 Set up a service graph to show the gun vacuum, i227 p4 against time and begin acquiring
data.
3 Heat the stainless steel pipework between the roughing port and the isolation valve,
including the ion pump, with a suitable heat gun.
4 Divide the pipework into approximately 150 mm sections (mark with indelible pen for
recording purposes).
5 Progressively heat each section along the pipework noting the time at which the heating
commenced (T0 when the service graphing was started) and the section being heated. Stop
heating for about 2 minutes between each section.
Note: Make sure not to heat the pipework above 60o C as the polymer seals will fail.
6 When all the pipework is heated, end the graph acquisition.
7 Compare the heating times of each section with the graph to see if there is an area of
heating that caused a greater change in pressure.
The above method can be extended to the input window, mode transformer and gun
envelope by opening the isolation valve and heating each section in turn.
Note: When heating the gun envelope, make sure to heat it slowly and uniformly and not more than 60o
C. Avoid direct heating of the potting compound.
6.12 Clearing gun end contamination
1 Close the isolation valve to isolate the waveguide from the gun end ion pump and
evacuation port.
2 Switch off the ion pump power supply.
3 Either use the purge kit or a new plastic bag filled with dry nitrogen (minimum cylinder
pressure 15 bar or mirror check for moisture – blow gas from cylinder onto a cool mirror
and check for fogging).
4 Open the roughing port and allow nitrogen into the waveguide.
5 Connect the turbo molecular roughing pump.
6 Start heating the stainless steel pipework between the roughing port and the isolation
valve, including the ion pump, with a suitable heat gun.
Note: Make sure not to heat the pipework above 60o C as the polymer seals will fail.
7 When the pipework has reached about 40o C, start the turbo molecular pump.
8 Continue to heat the pipework to approximately 50o C uniform temperature.
9 Keep running the turbo pump for about 90 minutes while trying to keep the pipework at
around 50o C.

Repairing the system
Beam conditioning
6.13 Beam conditioning
1 Select Xlow with the wedge out and 29980 monitor units.
2 Set the nominal dose rate to 6.
3 Set the field shape to Patient Plane Leakage.
4 Set Interlock Group 8.
5 Set the gun, magnetron tuner and the 2R servos to Manual.
6 Confirm the beam and start the machine.
Note: The PRF may be inhibited due to outgassing.
7 Set the Gun I Ctrl (Item 327) to 5.25 A.
8 Adjust the Magnetron Tuner Ctrl (Item 161) to a nominal frequency of 2858 MHz using an
RF wavemeter (approximately 220 turns).
9 If both vacuum monitors (Items 227 and 228) are better than -5.6, then increase the
nominal dose rate to the next step (i.e. 6, 12, 25, 50, 100, 200 and 400).
10 Repeat steps 8 and 9 until the nominal dose rate reaches 400. (RF only outgassing).
11 Set the nominal dose rate to 50.
12 Set the servos to auto.
13 If both vacuum monitors are better than -5.6, then increase the nominal dose rate to the
next step.
14 Repeat step 13 until nominal dose rate reaches 400.
15 Repeat for the remaining X-ray energies.
16 Repeat for all electron energies. Run for at least 1,000 MU.

After the six lines of status information, the IPCU sends a continuous output of diagnostic data

HVc sssss iiiiixA vvvvV F=ffff ppppmW i=yyyy v=zzz c=www d=hhhh

Item Description

www 12-bit hexadecimal control output

7.3.6 Formula to calculate the vacuum pressure from the Varian ion pump
